books.title: Aerodynamics for Paragliding Pilots
books.subtitle: Forces, profiles, glide and stability — the physics behind every flight.

chapters.title: Vectors and forces
chapters.subtitle: The language of physics for free flight
chapters.intro: Every force on your wing has a magnitude and a direction. Vectors are how pilots learn to combine those forces and reason about lift, drag and weight.

sections.text: What is a vector?

A vector represents a physical phenomenon that has three properties: a magnitude, a direction and a point of origin. In paragliding the two phenomena we constantly meet are force (measured in kg or Newtons) and speed (measured in km/h or m/s). To describe a force fully you must know how large it is — for example 10 kg or 16 kg — and you must also know where it is applied and which way it points, in two or three dimensions.

Graphically we draw a vector as an arrow. The length of the arrow represents the magnitude on a chosen scale: if the scale is 1 cm = 10 kg, then a 3 cm arrow corresponds to a force of 30 kg. The tail of the arrow — the end without the arrowhead — is the point of application, which for a force is the centre of the object that the force is acting on. The arrowhead shows the direction.

A worked example: a speed of 30 km/h applied to an object X, heading north-west. The arrow starts at the centre of X, points north-west, and its length on paper corresponds to 30 km/h on whatever scale we have chosen. With magnitude, direction and origin all specified, the speed is now fully described.

sections.caption: Adding two forces F1 and F4 graphically — direction matters as much as length.

sections.text: Adding vectors

When two vectors V1 and V2 act on the same object at the same time, the combined effect is the same as if a single resultant vector R were acting alone. This is called adding the two vectors. The resultant is found graphically, by the parallelogram (or tip-to-tail) construction — never by simply adding the lengths, because direction matters as much as magnitude.

Two special cases are easy. If two vectors have the same orientation and point the same way, the resultant has the sum of their magnitudes. If they have the same orientation but point in opposite directions, the resultant is the difference between them, oriented in the direction of the larger of the two.

sections.caption: F1 + F2 — parallel vectors in the same direction simply add.
F1 + F3 — opposite directions subtract.

sections.text: There is one strict rule about what you may add. You may only add vectors of the same type: two forces, or two speeds. You can never add a force and a speed, because the two phenomena are physically different and their magnitudes are not measured in the same units. Always check that the things you are combining are of the same kind before you draw the parallelogram.

sections.title: Remember
items: A vector has magnitude, direction and origin.
Add vectors graphically (parallelogram or tip-to-tail) — never just add the lengths.
Same direction: add magnitudes. Opposite direction: subtract.
Only add vectors of the same type — never a force with a speed.

chapters.title: Drag and the four factors
chapters.subtitle: Why your hand feels heavy out of a moving car
chapters.intro: Drag is the force the air exerts on any object moving through it. Only four things determine how big that force is — and the relationships are not all linear.

sections.text: Wind, relative wind and drag

Wind is air in motion relative to fixed objects on the ground — trees, houses, antennas. In the multiple-choice exam questions this is sometimes called 'airflow' or simply 'flow'. When you move through still air at speed V, the sensation is identical to standing still in a wind of the same speed coming from the opposite direction. This apparent wind, generated by your own motion, is called the relative wind.

Any object placed in a wind — whether real wind from a moving air mass or relative wind from your own motion — experiences a force called drag. Drag always points the same way as the wind: like the push you feel on your hand when you hold it out of a moving car, in the direction of the apparent airflow. Drag is expressed in kg or Newtons, with about 10 N corresponding to 1 kg of force.

sections.caption: Drag — the force air exerts on any body moving through it.

sections.text: Four factors, and only four

Drag depends on exactly four factors: the surface area exposed perpendicular to the wind, the wind speed, the density of the air and the shape of the object (its drag coefficient Cx). There are no other significant factors. In particular, weight, mass, humidity, dew point, air temperature, the pressure gradient and the molecular construction of the object's material all have no effect on drag at all.

The relationship between drag and exposed surface is linear: doubling the surface doubles the drag, halving it halves the drag. A worked example: an object in a 30 km/h wind at sea level has 300 N of drag at 2 m² of exposed area. Double the area to 4 m² and the drag doubles to 600 N. The fact that wind is 30 km/h and we are at sea level is irrelevant to the proportion — it appears in the exam only to test the strength of your knowledge.

sections.caption: A worked drag calculation — note the linear dependence on area and density.

sections.text: Air density has the same linear behaviour as surface area. If air density doubles, drag doubles. If it halves, drag halves. Two distinct halving altitudes are worth memorising: air pressure halves at roughly 5500 m AMSL, while air density only halves at about 6600 m AMSL — they are not the same number because temperature also changes with altitude. The next chapter covers the density-with-altitude table in detail.
Speed is squared

Wind speed enters with the square. Double the airspeed and drag is multiplied by four. Triple it and drag is multiplied by nine. Quadruple and drag is sixteen times larger. Halve the speed and drag drops to a quarter. A wing moving at 60 km/h experiences four times the drag it would at 30 km/h, with everything else equal. This is why a small change in speed produces such a big change in feel — and why pushing the bar feels so heavy.
Shape and the drag coefficient

The effect of shape on drag is captured by the drag coefficient Cx. A flat plate facing the wind has a Cx of about 1.0. A convex shape, like a hollow hemisphere with its open side into the wind, has a higher Cx of about 1.3 — about 30 percent more drag than the flat plate for the same frontal area. A streamlined teardrop body has a Cx of about 0.08 — more than twelve times less drag than the flat plate. A streamlined body misaligned with the wind has a Cx around 0.17. This is also the principle of an anemometer: half-shells catch much more wind on their open side than their convex back, so a wheel of half-shells spins reliably with the wind.

These coefficients have direct consequences for paragliding gear and posture. A pilot sitting upright behaves more like a flat plate; a pilot reclined and tucked behaves more like a streamlined body. The same logic applies to lines (round vs faired), to harnesses (pod vs open) and to the wing's own surface finish. Cleaner shapes give lower drag for the same frontal area.

sections.caption: Drag coefficients for typical shapes — from flat plate to streamlined body.

sections.text: How drag breaks down on a paraglider

On the flying paraglider system the total drag is roughly split four ways, as rules of thumb: about 20% profile drag (the wing's own skin and shape), about 20% line drag (the suspension lines), about 20% pilot/harness drag, and around 40% induced drag — the largest share, produced at the wingtips where the high-pressure air underneath spills around to the low-pressure side on top. Designers attack each share separately: more cells and cleaner stitching for profile drag, fewer and thinner lines for line drag, pod harnesses and a tucked seating position for pilot drag, and a high aspect ratio (long, slim wing) for induced drag. Every gain in performance comes with the trade-off of harder handling.

sections.title: Memorise
items: Drag = f(surface area, wind speed, air density, shape).
Linear in area and density; squared in speed.
Weight, humidity and temperature have no direct effect.
1 kg of force is roughly 10 Newtons.
Drag share on a paraglider: ~20% profile, ~20% lines, ~20% pilot, ~40% induced (the biggest).

chapters.title: Air density and altitude
chapters.subtitle: Why your wing flies faster up high
chapters.intro: Air thins as you climb. Because drag and lift both scale with density, every aerodynamic force you feel changes with altitude.

sections.text: Density falls with altitude

The density of the air — and the air pressure — decreases as altitude increases. The relationship is not perfectly linear: density falls more quickly in the lower layers of the atmosphere than higher up. As an aerodynamic body moves away from the Earth's surface, the drag acting on it decreases for the same airspeed and the same shape. The decrease happens faster in the lower layers than at high altitudes.

sections.caption: Air pressure (and density) versus altitude — fastest drop near the ground.

sections.text: Numbers worth memorising

For the theory exam there are four reference altitudes to memorise. At 1100, 2200, 3300 and 4400 metres above sea level, the air density relative to sea level is approximately 90, 80, 70 and 60 percent respectively. Because drag is linear in density, the drag of any object also drops to those same percentages at those altitudes, for the same shape and the same airspeed.

There is an easy memory aid: the altitude (in hundreds of metres) plus the density (in percent of sea-level value) always adds to 100. At 1100 m of altitude — that is, '11' on the hundreds-of-metres scale — the density is about 89 percent, or 11 + 89 ≈ 100. The trick works at every reference altitude in the table.

Lift behaves exactly like drag with respect to density: it is also linear in density. So a wing at 1100 m AMSL produces about 90 percent of the drag and 90 percent of the lift it would at sea level, all else being equal. To produce the same lift, the wing must therefore fly slightly faster at altitude. This is why launches at high altitude need a longer run.

sections.title: Two halving altitudes — don't confuse them
items: Air pressure halves at ~5500 m AMSL.
Air density halves at ~6600 m AMSL.
Both numbers can appear in the same multiple-choice question — read carefully.

sections.text: Why are the two halving altitudes different? Pressure depends only on the weight of the column of air above; that drops smoothly with altitude. Density depends on pressure AND temperature, and the temperature also falls with altitude — colder air at the same pressure is denser, which slows the density drop relative to the pressure drop. The result is the two distinct halving altitudes above. For the exam, the four-row density table at 1100, 2200, 3300 and 4400 m is what you need to memorise; the two halving altitudes are the trick question.

Two practical consequences follow. The first: stall speed rises with altitude. The wing has to fly faster to keep generating enough lift, so the minimum airspeed shifts up. The second: at altitude you can only compensate for the thinner air with speed — everything else (wing area, profile, your weight) stays the same. A useful estimate: if density halves, you do not need to run twice as fast, only by the square root of two (about 40 percent faster) because lift scales with the square of speed. In practice that means a noticeably longer run, on terrain that is often steep, snow-covered or tiring after the hike up. The upside: thinner air also forgives stronger wind on launch; a 30 km/h launch that would be marginal at 1500 m is sometimes possible at 3000 m for an experienced pilot.

sections.title: Memory aid
items: Altitude (hundreds of m) + density (%) = 100

chapters.title: Lift and the profile of a wing
chapters.subtitle: Why the same air produces both push and lift
chapters.intro: Place a flat object obliquely in the wind and two forces appear: drag along the wind, and lift perpendicular to it. A wing is just a clever shape that biases the balance hugely in favour of lift.

sections.text: Lift on a flat object

When an elongated, flat object such as a wing is held obliquely in the wind, two perpendicular forces act on it. The first is drag, parallel to the wind and in the same direction as the airflow — exactly as it would for any object. The second is lift, perpendicular to the wind, directed toward the upper surface of the object. Lift is what supports a paraglider in flight; drag is what costs us altitude as we fly.

Lift depends on the same four factors as drag: the exposed surface area, the wind speed, the air density and the shape-and-orientation of the object. The orientation contribution is captured by the angle of attack, the tilt of the wing relative to the airflow. There is an important nuance the exam likes to test: the surface profile itself (the curvature) is captured in the lift coefficient, while the size of the wing — its plan area — is the 'exposed surface'. These two are not the same.

sections.caption: Lift of a body — perpendicular to the airflow, toward the upper surface.

sections.text: The scaling laws are the same as for drag. If surface area or air density doubles or halves, lift and drag both scale linearly the same way. If the wind speed doubles, lift and drag are both multiplied by four, because both depend on the square of airspeed. This is the basis for the lift formula: lift depends on size, lift coefficient, air density and the square of airspeed.
Three principles of lift

Aerodynamicists describe lift with three complementary explanations, all of which apply simultaneously to a paraglider. The exam doesn't ask you to derive any formulas, but you should recognise each of these on multiple-choice answers.

Bernoulli's principle: the upper surface is more curved than the lower surface, which forces the air on top to flow faster. Faster flow gives lower pressure; the slower flow underneath gives higher pressure. The difference creates a net upward force on the wing — the suction on top is roughly twice as strong as the push underneath.

Newton's recoil principle (action-reaction): a wing at a positive angle of attack deflects the airflow downward as it passes; the equal and opposite reaction is an upward force on the wing. You can feel exactly the same effect if you hold your hand flat out of a moving car and slowly tilt it: tilt one way and the hand is pushed up, tilt the other way and it is pushed down.

Circulation flow: at the sharp trailing edge of the wing a starting vortex develops, which sets up a steady circulation of air around the wing. The wake is deflected downward and the reaction is an additional upward force. These three explanations are not competing theories — they are three views of the same physics, and modern aerodynamics combines all three.
Where the lift comes from

Lift is the net result of pressure differences on the upper and lower surfaces of the wing. On the upper surface there is suction — a negative pressure, as if a vacuum cleaner were pulling on the wing. On the lower surface there is positive pressure, as if a fan were blowing on it. The two combine into a net upward force perpendicular to the airflow.

These two contributions are not equal. The suction on the upper surface is about twice as strong as the positive pressure on the lower surface. At an angle of attack of about 10°, the distribution of total lift is roughly two thirds on the upper surface and one third on the lower. This is one reason why a wing with damaged upper-surface coating loses lift much more quickly than one with damage underneath.

Lift is also unevenly distributed front-to-back along the chord. Roughly two thirds of the total lift is generated by the front third of the wing. This is why the leading-edge area carries the heaviest stress, why the A risers carry more load than the others in trim flight, and why the front cells must be the most robustly built. The wingtips also produce spiral air movements — vortices — where the high pressure underneath leaks around to the lower pressure on top, adding induced drag and turbulence in the wake.
The shape of the profile

The profile of a wing is the shape of the cross-section from leading edge to trailing edge. Modern paraglider profiles are quite thick and strongly asymmetric. The upper surface is markedly curved, particularly over the anterior third of the wing; the lower surface is only slightly convex. Slow aircraft like paragliders use thick, asymmetric profiles to generate lift at low launch speed; fast aircraft like fighter jets use much thinner, flatter, less asymmetric profiles.

sections.caption: A typical asymmetric wing profile in cross-section.

sections.text: Key geometric points of the profile are the leading edge (the front), the trailing edge (the rear), the mean chord (the line from leading to trailing edge) and the angle of attack — the angle between the chord and the direction of the relative airflow (NOT the angle to the horizon — a paraglider is always sinking slightly, so the relative wind comes at a slight upward slope). Important aerodynamic points include the stagnation point on the leading edge (where the flow divides between upper and lower surfaces), the centre of thrust (where the resultant aerodynamic force effectively acts) and the separation point on the upper surface (where the flow detaches at high angles of attack). All of these are covered in the next chapter. One practical hint already: the cell openings on a paraglider sit on the underside of the leading edge precisely because in normal flight the stagnation point lies there, so air enters the cells naturally.

sections.title: Lift facts
items: Lift depends on size, lift coefficient, air density and wind speed.
Doubling airspeed quadruples lift (and drag).
Three explanations (all true at once): Bernoulli (pressure difference), Newton (action-reaction), circulation flow (starting vortex).
At ~10° AoA: 2/3 of lift on the upper surface, 1/3 on the lower.
Front 1/3 of the wing carries roughly 2/3 of the total lift.

chapters.title: Angle of attack and the polar of forces
chapters.subtitle: Why a single control input changes everything
chapters.intro: The angle of attack — the tilt of the wing relative to the airflow — sets every aerodynamic outcome. From best glide to stall, the same wing behaves like a different aircraft as this one angle changes.

sections.text: Stagnation point and separation point

On the leading edge of the wing there is a special point called the stagnation point. This is where the airflow divides between the air that will travel along the upper surface and the air that will travel along the lower surface. The stagnation point is aerodynamic — it is not a fixed mark on the wing. It moves as the angle of attack changes.

Further back on the upper surface lies the separation point. This is the place where the airflow detaches from the wing's upper surface. Behind it the smooth flow gives way to turbulence; lift in that region collapses and drag rises. Like the stagnation point, the separation point moves as the angle of attack changes — and at very high angles it walks far enough forward that the wing as a whole stops flying.

sections.caption: Stagnation point on the leading edge and separation point on the upper surface.

sections.text: When the (positive) angle of attack of a wing increases, the stagnation point on the lower surface moves toward the leading edge. When the angle of attack decreases, the stagnation point moves toward the trailing edge. Pilots cannot see the stagnation point directly, but its movement is the underlying mechanism that produces the changing pressures and forces you feel through the risers.

sections.caption: How the stagnation point shifts as angle of attack changes.

sections.text: Four regimes of angle of attack

At a moderate angle of attack — about 10–15° — the wing is in its optimum operating range. There is strong lift from the upper-surface suction and consistent positive pressure underneath. The drag is reduced relative to lift, and the lift-to-drag ratio is near its maximum.

Increase the angle of attack further and the separation point on the upper surface walks forward. Behind it, turbulent flow replaces the negative pressure with a downward component. Lift drops, drag rises, and the wing slows down. Push past about 20–25° and the wing stalls — lift collapses, the wing no longer flies and it sinks.

At a zero angle of attack the wing still has some drag but produces very little lift: the suction on top and the slight suction underneath largely cancel. With a negative angle of attack the situation flips. The upper surface gets positive pressure and the lower surface gets negative pressure. Both forces now act downward, and the wing produces negative lift.

sections.caption: Angle of attack increases — flow begins to separate on the upper surface.

sections.text: The polar of forces

The polar of forces is a graph that plots the lift coefficient against the drag coefficient as the angle of attack changes. Each point on the curve corresponds to a particular angle of attack; the values shown are orders of magnitude for illustration, not precise measurements. The ratio of lift to drag — the slope from the origin to a point on the curve — depends primarily on the angle of attack.

At about 10° the lift-to-drag ratio is at its maximum: this is the best glide angle. Increasing the angle of attack further increases lift but increases drag even more, so the ratio degrades. At about 20° lift reaches its maximum value, but with relatively high drag — this corresponds to minimum sink. At about 25° lift collapses entirely: the wing stalls.

Knowing the polar means you can predict the consequence of any pilot input. Reducing the angle of attack by 2° from best glide increases the airspeed (the wing flies faster). Increasing the angle of attack by 2° from best glide decreases the airspeed. From this single curve you can read off every flight regime the wing offers.

sections.title: Three angles to remember
items: ~10° → best lift-to-drag ratio → best glide.
~20° → maximum lift → minimum sink, but slow.
~25° → stall: airflow detaches, wing no longer flies.

chapters.title: Wing geometry and wing loading
chapters.subtitle: Span, area, chord, aspect ratio, payload
chapters.intro: A wing is described by a handful of numbers. Knowing what they mean — and how flat and projected values differ — lets you read a spec sheet and predict how a glider will fly.

sections.text: Wingspan and area

Wingspan is the distance between the two wingtips, measured in metres. We distinguish the flat (actual) wingspan, measured on a wing spread out on the floor, from the projected wingspan, measured on the shadow of the inflated wing. The flat wingspan is always greater than the projected wingspan, because an inflated paraglider is curved into an arc and its tips are pulled inward and down.

sections.caption: Wingspan — left-to-right distance between the wingtips.

sections.text: Area is the total surface of the wing, expressed in square metres. As with wingspan, there is a flat area (measured on the floor) and a projected area (the shadow of the inflated wing). The flat area is always greater than or equal to the projected area; only for a very flat wing are they nearly the same. Aerodynamically the projected area is what counts in flight, because that is the area actually held into the airflow above the pilot.
Mean chord and the formula S = p × e

The mean chord (average depth) p of a wing is the average distance from leading edge to trailing edge, expressed in metres. There is a simple relationship between area S, wingspan e and mean chord p: area equals mean chord multiplied by wingspan, or S = p × e. So mean chord p = S / e.

Worked example: a wing with a 10 m wingspan and a 25 m² area has a mean chord of 25 / 10 = 2.5 m. A wing with a 10 m wingspan and a 12.5 m² area has a mean chord of 12.5 / 10 = 1.25 m. The second wing is considerably narrower — and as we will see, has a higher aspect ratio.

sections.caption: Chord and mean chord — the average depth of the wing.

sections.text: Aspect ratio

Aspect ratio is the ratio of wingspan to mean chord, which is equivalent to wingspan squared divided by area: aspect ratio = e² / S. The latter formula is the most practical one for calculation. We can speak of flat aspect ratio (using flat span and flat area) or projected aspect ratio (using projected values).

sections.caption: Aspect ratio — the higher the number, the slimmer the wing.
Projected area — the shadow of the inflated wing.

sections.text: Paragliders typically have an aspect ratio of about 5; hang gliders are typically around 8. A high aspect ratio means a long, narrow wing — more performance, primarily because it REDUCES induced drag. Induced drag is generated at the wingtip, where the high pressure underneath spills around to the suction above; the longer and narrower the wing, the smaller the proportion of the span that is affected by that wingtip leakage. This is the same idea behind aeroplane winglets: anything that makes the effective wing 'longer and slimmer' for the same area reduces induced drag. The cost of a higher aspect ratio is that the wing becomes more demanding to fly — collapses are more violent and recovery less spontaneous. With experience pilots can recognise a high-aspect-ratio wing at a glance: long and slim, narrow chord. Lower aspect-ratio wings are wider, shorter span — usually more forgiving.

Two short calculations: a paraglider of 10 m span and 25 m² area has aspect ratio 100/25 = 4. A wing of 10 m span and 12.5 m² has 100/12.5 = 8. The slimmer the wing, the higher the aspect ratio. Weight and load data sometimes given in questions are irrelevant to the aspect ratio — they only test whether you know what matters.
Take-off weight and wing loading

Take-off weight is the total of everything in the air: pilot, harness, wing and anything carried. The wing load is the take-off weight minus the wing's own weight — the load the wing has to support in the air. For a typical paraglider with a 5 kg wing and a 70–95 kg pilot+gear load, the take-off weight is 75–100 kg. The simplest practical method: stand on the bathroom scale with your full flight kit (harness, reserve, instruments, food, water, hiking shoes) AND your wing in the pack — that figure is your take-off weight.

sections.caption: Payload, all-up weight and wing loading.

sections.text: Wing loading is the take-off weight divided by the (usually projected) wing area, expressed in kg/m². For paragliders the typical range is 2.5 to 4 kg/m². For a 25 m² wing with the loads above, you get 75/25 = 3 kg/m² at the minimum and 100/25 = 4 kg/m² at the maximum. A heavier loading shifts the polar curve up and to the right — the wing flies faster at every angle of attack, including a higher stall speed. A more heavily loaded wing is faster and more agile; a lightly loaded wing is slower, more sluggish and less demanding to fly.

Wing torsion (also called washout) is a deliberate variation in angle of attack between different sections of the span. It generally improves the wing's stability and reduces the abruptness of stalls — the tips keep flying when the centre is approaching its limit. Wing torsion is one reason why modern wings can be flown closer to their stall point without an instant catastrophic departure.

sections.title: Geometry crib
items: Aspect ratio = wingspan² / area = wingspan / mean chord.
Paragliders ~5, hang gliders ~8.
Higher aspect ratio = LOWER induced drag = better performance (but harder to fly).
Wing loading = take-off weight / projected area, ~2.5–4 kg/m² for paragliders.
Heavier load = faster, more agile; lighter load = slower, less demanding.
Flat values always ≥ projected values.

chapters.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio
chapters.subtitle: Why a paraglider in trim is in balance
chapters.intro: In steady flight every force on a paraglider must cancel out. Once you see that balance, the famous "glide ratio" is just geometry.

sections.text: Equilibrium in straight flight

A paraglider in steady, unaccelerated flight is moving uniformly along a straight trajectory. By the definition of equilibrium, the resultant of all forces acting on the wing is zero. The total weight in flight — pilot, harness, wing and everything carried (PTV) — acts vertically downward and must be exactly cancelled by a vertically upward force of equal magnitude. That force is called the resultant aerodynamic force (FRA).

The resultant aerodynamic force can be decomposed into two components. Lift is perpendicular to the trajectory and points upward. Drag is parallel to the trajectory and points opposite to the direction of motion. The trajectory itself is along the direction of the relative airflow — the wind the wing experiences as it flies.

sections.caption: Forces on a paraglider — weight balanced by the resultant aerodynamic force.
Forces on a paraglider in the relative airflow frame.

sections.text: There is also a component of weight parallel to the trajectory, pointing toward the leading edge of the wing. This component opposes drag and is called the thrust. In steady flight thrust and drag are equal: that is what makes the trajectory remain straight at uniform speed. The glide angle α is the angle between the trajectory and the horizon; by simple geometry, α is identical to the angle α' between the lift vector and the resultant aerodynamic force.
Glide ratio — four equivalent definitions

The glide ratio is one of the most important characteristics of a wing. The greater it is, the better the glider performs — it travels further for each metre of altitude it loses. The glide ratio of current paragliders is about 8–9 in still air; modern competition wings can be higher. Hang gliders are typically higher still.

sections.caption: Glide angle vs glide ratio — a geometric pair of complementary numbers.

sections.text: There are four equivalent ways to compute the glide ratio. First, glide ratio equals horizontal distance travelled D divided by altitude lost H: glide = D/H. Second, since both happen over the same time, glide ratio equals horizontal speed divided by sink rate. Third, because the force triangle and the trajectory triangle are similar, glide ratio equals lift divided by drag (P/T). Fourth, since lift and drag are proportional to their respective coefficients, glide ratio equals Cz / Cx.

In summary: glide ratio = D/H = horizontal speed / sink rate = P/T = Cz/Cx. The glide ratio is dimensionless — it does not depend on the units you use, only on the geometric efficiency of the wing. A glide ratio of 8 means the wing travels 8 m horizontally for every 1 m it descends in still air.
Worked numbers

Some quick examples. A sailplane with glide ratio 8 flying at 800 m above ground can travel 8 × 0.8 km = 6.4 km in still air. A wing with glide ratio 12 from 2400 m can travel 12 × 2.4 = 28.8 km. A glider that descended 900 m while traveling the maximum 5.4 km has a glide ratio of 5.4/0.9 = 6. A glider flying at 9 m/s with a 1.5 m/s sink rate has a glide ratio of 9/1.5 = 6.
How drag and angle relate to glide ratio

If the drag of a glider decreases — even if lift is unchanged — the resultant aerodynamic force increases, the angles α' and α both decrease, and the glide ratio (P/T = D/H) increases. Conversely, if drag increases, the glide ratio decreases and the glide angle increases. A larger glide angle therefore always corresponds to a smaller glide ratio: the steeper you descend, the less far you go.

sections.caption: Glide angle: the steeper it is, the smaller the glide ratio.

sections.title: Worked examples
items: Glide ratio 8 from 800 m → 6.4 km of distance.
Distance 5.4 km from 900 m → glide ratio 6.
Glide 10 at 12 m/s horizontal → sink 1.2 m/s.
Glide 9 at 1 m/s sink → ground speed 9 m/s.

chapters.title: Polar curve of speeds
chapters.subtitle: Best glide, minimum sink, full bar — and what they cost
chapters.intro: Every paraglider has a single curve that tells you what it does at every angle of attack. Reading it is the key to choosing the right speed for the conditions.

sections.text: What the polar shows

The horizontal (airspeed) and vertical (sink) velocities of a wing — and their ratio, the glide ratio — all vary according to the angle of attack. Decreasing the angle of attack by nose-diving the wing increases horizontal speed. Increasing the angle of attack first slows the wing; at very high angles the horizontal speed continues to drop while the sink rate begins to rise again — towards the stall.

The polar curve of speeds is a graphical representation of horizontal (air speed) and vertical (sink rate) velocities over the entire speed range of a paraglider, from minimum to maximum. The horizontal axis is airspeed, increasing to the right; the vertical axis is sink rate, growing downward. Every angle of attack the wing can fly corresponds to one point on the curve.

sections.caption: A paraglider polar curve — airspeed on the horizontal axis, sink rate downward.

sections.text: Four key points on the polar

Four points on the polar curve matter. Stall (point d, full brake): the lowest horizontal speed; just below it the wing no longer flies and sinks vertically. Minimum sink Tmin (point at the apex of the curve, light brake): the horizontal speed at which the sink rate is smallest — the longest air time. In the textbook example, Tmin is between 7 and 8 m/s with a sink rate of about 1.5 m/s.

Best glide Fmax (hands up, near trim): the point on the curve where the tangent from the origin touches it. No other tangent line through the origin can produce a smaller glide angle, so this is the speed that gives the maximum glide ratio. In the textbook example, Fmax is about 9 m/s with a sink rate of about 1.7 m/s — a ratio of about 5.3.

Maximum speed Vmax (full speedbar): the rightmost point of the curve, where the horizontal speed is largest. The glide ratio is poor at Vmax — in the textbook example, 13 m/s horizontal speed with 3.5 m/s sink, a glide ratio of only 3.7 — but the speed is useful when fighting headwind or escaping sink.

sections.caption: The four key points: stall, minimum sink, best glide and Vmax.
Best glide point: tangent from origin touches the polar.

sections.text: Wing loading shifts the polar

Wing loading modifies the polar curve. A higher loading means the resultant aerodynamic force must be larger to balance flight. Because the aerodynamic force grows with the square of airspeed, the wing must fly faster at every angle of attack. The whole polar curve shifts up and to the right: stall speed, minimum sink speed, best glide speed and maximum speed all increase together.

The glide ratio at each angle of attack does not change with wing loading — only the absolute speeds shift. This is why a 95 kg pilot on the same wing as a 70 kg pilot flies faster everywhere, with the same glide ratio at corresponding angles of attack. The exception is at extreme loadings, outside the certified range, where the wing profile starts to deform.
Flying in moving air

In still air the polar is read directly. In wind, the curve relative to the air mass never changes — what changes is the curve relative to the ground. A headwind shifts the apparent origin to the right (you give up some ground speed at every airspeed). The best-glide tangent now touches the curve at a higher airspeed: to fly the maximum distance into the wind you must accelerate.

sections.caption: Polar with headwind — apparent origin shifts; fly faster for best glide.
Flying with wind and vertical air movement — speed-to-fly logic.

sections.text: With a tailwind, the apparent origin shifts to the left and the same logic shows you must fly slower, closer to the minimum sink speed, to achieve the best glide ratio over the ground. Some quick numbers: a wing in a 6 m/s headwind needs about 11 m/s airspeed to maximise glide; a wing flying at 15 m/s with 2 m/s sink, hitting a 5 m/s headwind, sees no change in sink rate but loses 5 m/s of ground speed — and the glide ratio over the ground drops accordingly.
Vertical air movement

Vertical air movement works the same way as horizontal wind, but on the vertical axis. Sinking air shifts the origin downward; to maintain best glide ratio over the ground, again fly faster. A wing at a given angle of attack does not change its airspeed when the air mass rises or sinks — only the rate at which it moves relative to the ground changes. In lift, fly minimum sink to spend the longest possible time in the rising air.

sections.caption: Glide ratio and glide angle when flying in wind.

sections.title: Speed to fly
items: Calm air, max distance → best glide (hands up).
Calm air, max time in air → minimum sink (slight brake).
Headwind or sink → fly faster (accelerator).
Tailwind or lift → slow down (less or no speedbar).

chapters.title: Axes, stability and turning flight
chapters.subtitle: Pitch, roll, yaw — and the load factor in a turn
chapters.intro: A paraglider can rotate about three axes. In a turn the centrifugal force adds to your weight, increasing the load on the wing and your minimum flying speed.

sections.text: The three axes

A paraglider can have rotary motion about three principal axes. Motion about the vertical axis is called yaw — a rotation in plan view that swings the wingtips forward and backward as the wing rotates around a vertical line through the pilot. Motion about the longitudinal axis (running front-to-back through the pilot) is called roll — a sideways motion that tips one wingtip down and the other up; wingovers are essentially a roll motion. Motion about the transverse axis (running left-right) is called pitch — a forward/backward motion of the wing across the pilot's body. A wing entering a thermal pitches up; on the exit it pitches forward.

sections.caption: Motion around the three axes — pitch, roll and yaw.
Roll about the longitudinal axis.
Pitch about the transverse axis.
Yaw about the vertical axis.

sections.text: Stable, indifferent, unstable

A glider is normally constructed so that, when no control is applied, it flies straight at a uniform speed — balanced flight. When a force such as a pilot input or an external disturbance such as turbulence temporarily disturbs the glider, a stable glider spontaneously returns to its original straight uniform flight at the normal angle of attack. For example, a wing that returns from accelerated flight back to its trim speed when you release the speed bar, without any pilot input, is showing stable behaviour.

Two other behaviours are possible. A glider that increases its speed by itself, after straight uniform flight, with no pilot input, has unstable characteristics. A glider that keeps whatever flight state the pilot initiated, but then does not spontaneously return when input is released, has indifferent (neutral) characteristics.

sections.caption: Stable, unstable and indifferent behaviour.
Stability illustrated — the ball-in-bowl analogy.

sections.title: Ball-in-bowl analogy
items: Stable wing = ball in a bowl: a push and it oscillates back to the middle.
Unstable wing = ball on an upturned bowl: a push and it runs away — accelerating frontal collapse.
Indifferent wing = ball on a flat surface: a push and it holds the new state forever (e.g. stays in a tight spiral).

sections.text: Stability can be defined separately about each of the three axes. A wing that rolls in calm air without input is unstable about the longitudinal axis. A wing that changes its angle of attack by itself is unstable about its glide path. A wing that yaws spontaneously without input is unstable about the vertical axis. A small number of paragliders are described as 'indifferent in a tight spiral' — exit from a steady tight 360° turn is not necessarily spontaneous and may require active pilot intervention.
Centrifugal force and load factor

In a stabilised turn, an additional horizontal force is added to the total weight. This is the centrifugal force, directed outward, away from the centre of the turn. The vertical weight P and the horizontal centrifugal force Fc share the same point of application, so we can add them as vectors. The resultant R — also called the effective weight — is directed downward and outward, larger than P, and tilted at the bank angle.

sections.caption: Turning flight — centrifugal force adds to weight.

sections.text: The load factor is the ratio of the resultant R to the straight-flight weight P, expressed in G (multiples of normal gravity). If P = 100 kg and R = 250 kg, the load factor is 250 / 100 = 2.5 G. The numbers grow rapidly with bank angle. At 30° bank the increase is barely noticeable. At 45° the load factor is about 1.4 G and the speed range is multiplied by about 1.2. At 60°: 2 G and 1.4×. At 70°: 3 G. At 80° the theoretical load factor reaches 6 G.
Why the wing flies faster in a turn

In a stabilised turn the resultant aerodynamic force FRA must exactly oppose R. The sharper the turn, the larger R and the larger FRA. Because aerodynamic force scales with the square of airspeed, the wing's airspeed must increase to produce a larger FRA — though not as fast as the load factor itself increases (the relationship goes with the square root).

The whole speed range shifts upward in a turn. Minimum (stall) speed rises along with cruise speed and Vmax. Transitioning from straight flight into a stabilised turn therefore increases both the wing loading and the minimum flying speed. A common misconception is that a very fast steep turn cannot stall — it can. Higher loading just means you need MORE speed to stay above the (also-higher) stall speed. Over-braking in a steeply banked turn is dangerous precisely because the inside wing approaches its now-higher stall speed quickly and can drop into a spin.
Accelerated flight — never brake on the bar

Trim flight leaves the brake line slightly bowed: there are 10–15 cm of slack at the trailing edge. When the speed bar is pushed, the leading edge is pulled down and the trailing edge rises, so the brake line becomes taut. In that state, pulling the brake briefly raises the trailing edge above its accelerated position and creates a strong local angle of attack spike while the rest of the wing is at a very low overall AoA. The result is a significantly increased collapse risk — typically frontal, sometimes asymmetric — with a more violent recovery because the wing is flying fast.

sections.title: Accelerated-flight rule
items: On the speed bar: never steer with the brake.
Steer with weight shift first.
If that is not enough, add a light pull on the rear risers (1–2 cm).
Come off the bar at altitude before landing in turbulent air.

books.title: Meteorology for Free Flight
books.subtitle: Atmosphere, clouds, wind systems, fronts and Alpine weather.

chapters.title: The atmosphere and atmospheric pressure
chapters.subtitle: What we are flying through
chapters.intro: Our flying happens in a very thin shell of mixed gases. Knowing its layers, its composition and how its pressure behaves is the foundation of every weather decision.

sections.text: Layers of the atmosphere

The atmosphere is the envelope of air that surrounds the Earth. It is densest at sea level and thins out gradually until it reaches the vacuum of space. Meteorologists divide it into several layers. For paragliding we only need to concern ourselves with the troposphere — the layer in direct contact with the Earth, where essentially all weather happens. The higher layers (stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere) do not concern us directly.

sections.caption: Layers of the atmosphere — paragliders fly entirely in the troposphere.
The troposphere — almost all weather phenomena occur here.

sections.text: The troposphere is bounded above by the tropopause. In the northern latitudes this is at around 11,000 m above sea level. The tropopause is slightly lower in winter, when the air is colder and denser, and slightly higher in summer when the air is warmer. The exact height also depends on latitude — it is lower over the poles and higher over the equator.

sections.caption: A thin atmospheric shell relative to the Earth.

sections.text: What air is made of

Dry air is about 78 percent nitrogen and 21 percent oxygen. The remaining 1 percent is made up of carbon dioxide, water vapour and rare gases such as argon and helium. Suspended in this mix are variable amounts of water vapour, dust, pollen and aerosols — the ingredients of cloud, mist and pollution.

sections.caption: Composition of the troposphere — roughly 78/21 nitrogen/oxygen.
Troposphere — basic facts about the layer that contains our weather.

sections.text: Atmospheric pressure

Atmospheric pressure is due to the effect of gravity on the air mass — it is the weight of the column of air pressing down. It is typically measured in hectopascals (hPa); one atmosphere is approximately 1000 hPa. The standard reference value at sea level is 1013.25 hPa, with a corresponding standard temperature of 15 °C and an average temperature gradient of 0.65 °C per 100 m.

sections.caption: Air pressure — the weight of the air column above us.

sections.text: Because air is a compressible gas, the higher you go the lower the pressure. The decrease is not linear. Two reference points are useful to remember. At 5500 m AMSL the atmospheric pressure is about half its sea-level value. At 11000 m the pressure is about a quarter of sea level. So if sea-level pressure is 980 hPa, at 5500 m it would be 490 hPa.

sections.caption: Standard atmosphere — how pressure changes with altitude.

sections.text: There is also a useful gas-law relationship: if pressure halves, volume doubles. A balloon of 5 dm³ at sea level rises to 5500 m (pressure halved) and becomes 10 dm³. Take it to 11000 m (pressure quartered) and it becomes 20 dm³. Altimeters use the standard atmosphere as a reference: pressure 1013.25 hPa at sea level, temperature 15 °C, lapse rate 0.65 °C / 100 m.

Pressure at a given location is not constant over time. Warming and cooling of land surfaces, and the movement of air masses around the Earth, make pressure rise and fall. This is why pressure readings on weather maps are always corrected to sea level (QNH) so they can be compared between locations at different altitudes.

sections.caption: Standard atmosphere — the reference for altimetry.

sections.title: Atmosphere essentials
items: Troposphere = the layer that contains essentially all weather.
Tropopause: ~11000 m at our latitudes.
78 % nitrogen, 21 % oxygen, 1 % trace gases (plus variable water).
Standard pressure at sea level = 1013.25 hPa.
Pressure halves at 5500 m, quartered at 11000 m.

chapters.title: How the air really warms up
chapters.subtitle: The Sun doesn't heat the air directly
chapters.intro: Most pilots are surprised the first time they hear it: the Sun's rays barely warm the atmosphere. They warm the ground, and the ground warms the air — that detour is what makes thermals possible.

sections.text: An indirect heating process

The Sun does not warm the atmosphere directly. Solar radiation passes through the air with very little absorption. It is absorbed by the ground, which warms up; the ground then warms the layer of air immediately above it by conduction and convection. Local pockets of this warmed air, less dense than their cooler surroundings, become buoyant and rise as thermals.

sections.caption: Standard atmosphere — the Sun warms ground first, air second.
Convection and air density — warm air is less dense and rises.

sections.text: Which ground heats best

Not all ground heats the air equally. Dark, dry, rough ground (rock, soil, asphalt) absorbs sunlight efficiently and warms the air strongly. Wet ground — swamps, irrigated fields, forests with damp soil — wastes much of the incoming energy on evaporating water, which absorbs heat instead of warming the air. Smooth, bright surfaces such as snow or pale rock reflect a large fraction of the sunlight straight back without absorbing it: they are poor thermal generators.

The orientation of the surface relative to the Sun also matters enormously. Strong thermals develop where solar radiation arrives essentially perpendicular to the surface — mountain faces in the morning, southern slopes around noon, western slopes in the afternoon. A surface that catches grazing light at a low angle absorbs much less energy than one with the Sun overhead.

sections.caption: Thermals — bubbles of warmer air released from the ground.

sections.text: The dry adiabatic lapse rate

An air parcel that moves vertically without exchanging heat with its surroundings is said to behave adiabatically. As long as no water condenses inside it, a rising adiabatic air parcel cools by 1 °C for every 100 m of altitude gained — and warms by the same amount on the way down. This 1 °C / 100 m dry adiabatic lapse rate is a constant property of dry air; it does not depend on the ambient temperature.

The reason for the cooling is that, as the parcel rises into thinner air, it expands against the lower pressure outside. Expansion uses up the parcel's internal energy and the temperature drops. The reverse holds on descent: compression converts work back into temperature, and the parcel warms.

sections.caption: Dry adiabatic — 1 °C per 100 m of vertical motion.
Decompression on the way up — expansion cools the parcel.

sections.text: Ambient temperature and the emagram

The actual temperature of the atmosphere at each altitude is not constant. It depends on time of day, recent weather, and the path the air mass has taken. An emagram (temperature-altitude diagram) plots measured ambient temperature against altitude. The contrast between this measured ambient curve and the constant 1 °C / 100 m adiabatic line determines whether the air is stable or unstable, and how high thermals can climb.

The standard atmosphere has an average lapse rate of 0.65 °C per 100 m for the lower troposphere. For strong thermals to develop, the actual lapse rate near the ground should be even steeper than this — roughly 0.6 to 0.8 °C per 100 m between the surface and around 3000 m, with no strong inversion above. A thermal stops rising when its adiabatically-cooled temperature matches the ambient temperature — at that altitude there is no longer a buoyancy difference.

sections.caption: Thermal top — the level where the parcel matches its surroundings.
Adiabatic processes — how an air parcel changes with altitude.

sections.title: Lapse rates
items: Dry adiabatic = 1 °C / 100 m (constant).
Standard atmosphere ≈ 0.65 °C / 100 m.
Strong thermals need a steep ambient lapse rate near the ground.
Thermal top: where parcel temperature meets ambient temperature.

chapters.title: Inversions, isothermal layers and stability
chapters.subtitle: Why thermals stop where they stop
chapters.intro: A rising thermal eventually runs into a layer that's just as warm or warmer than it is. Inversions and isothermal layers are the invisible ceilings of every flying day.

sections.text: When the air gets warmer with altitude

An inversion is a layer in which temperature increases with altitude instead of decreasing. An isothermal layer is one in which temperature stays the same — a constant temperature with height. Both kinds of layers are stable to vertical motion. A rising air parcel that adiabatically cools at 1 °C / 100 m reaches such a layer and finds itself colder (and therefore denser) than its surroundings; it stops climbing.

Stable stratification is the meteorological term for an air layer in which vertical motion is suppressed. For a pilot it means the same thing as 'no thermals beyond this altitude'. Sunny weather at altitude with fog in the valleys is a strong sign of a stable layer — typically an anticyclonic morning with descending air and a ground inversion below. Inversions are most dramatic in winter: it can be cold and foggy at the lake in Zurich while skiers on the peaks sit in shirtsleeves in warm sun.

sections.caption: Stable layering — inversion or isothermal layer caps the thermals.
An inversion: temperature rises with height instead of falling.

sections.text: Ground (radiation) inversion

On a clear, calm night the ground radiates its stored heat away to space as infrared and cools much faster than the air above it. By dawn there is often a shallow layer near the ground that is colder than the air higher up — a ground or radiation inversion. The inversion blocks thermals at low altitude until the morning sun has warmed the ground enough to dissolve it. The thicker the inversion the longer it takes to dissolve, sometimes lasting into the early afternoon in autumn and winter.

Radiation fog frequently forms inside this nocturnal cold layer when the temperature drops below the dew point. It is typical of clear, calm autumn and winter nights and dissolves once enough sun has warmed the ground from above. A pilot taking off in the early morning into clear air at altitude with fog in the valleys below is flying above an inversion: dramatic but stable conditions, with little thermal activity until the inversion goes away.
Reading the emagram

An emagram (or radiosonde sounding) plots actual ambient temperature against altitude. Reading one is largely about spotting flat or reversed sections that will cap the day's flying. A normal positive lapse rate of 0.6 to 0.8 °C per 100 m means good thermal potential. A zero (isothermal) or negative (inversion) gradient at any altitude is a ceiling — thermals will stop there.

Typical exam-style emagram questions list ambient temperatures at successive altitudes (e.g. 500 m → 15 °C, 800 m → 16 °C, …). Walk up the list: where temperature increases with altitude you have an inversion; where it stays constant you have an isothermal layer; where the drop is well under 1 °C per 100 m you have a stable layer; where it is close to 1 °C per 100 m the layer is unstable and rich in thermals. In Switzerland the only radiosonde station is Payerne, which launches morning and midday balloons.
Reverse thermal (Umkehrthermik)

Two different phenomena are sometimes both called 'reverse thermal' in flying circles. The first is the descending counterpart of a normal thermal: cold air sliding down a shaded slope after the sun has gone off it, or cold air pooling at a valley floor as the daily cycle reverses. For a pilot this appears as a steady downflow on slopes that should be lifting — a useful sign that the thermal day is ending.

The second meaning is a convergence updraft that can develop in the evening, when the cold mountain-wind drainage from both sides of a valley meets in the middle and squeezes the warmer valley-floor air upward. This produces a brief but flyable lift along the valley centre line (a well-known example is the lift above Interlaken at dusk), persisting for tens of minutes until the warm air mass at the floor is exhausted.

sections.title: Caps to know
items: Inversion: temperature INCREASES with altitude.
Isothermal: temperature CONSTANT with altitude.
Both are stable: thermals stop at them.
Ground inversion forms overnight, dissolves with morning sun. Winter inversions can last all day with valley fog + sunny peaks.
Reverse thermal can mean either downflow on shaded slopes or evening convergence updraft along a valley centre.

chapters.title: Water in the air
chapters.subtitle: Dew point, latent heat and the moist adiabatic
chapters.intro: Water vapour is invisible — until it isn't. The phase changes of water are the engine of clouds, condensation heat and the moist adiabatic lapse rate.

sections.text: Three phases, six phase changes

Water exists in three physical states: ice (solid), water (liquid) and water vapour (gas). Water vapour is invisible — perfectly transparent in air. What we casually call 'steam' is actually a hot cloud of suspended water droplets, not vapour. Mist, frost, dew, hail and snow are all common condensed or solid forms of water in the atmosphere.

Changing between these three phases either absorbs energy or releases it. To go from ice to water (melting), from water to vapour (evaporation), or from ice straight to vapour (sublimation), the water must absorb energy in the form of heat to overcome the intermolecular forces. In the opposite direction — condensation, freezing or deposition (vapour straight to solid) — energy is released into the surroundings as latent heat.

sections.caption: Latent heat of condensation — phase change releases energy.
Latent heat — the energy gained or lost by phase change.

sections.text: Saturation and dew point

Humidity is the amount of water vapour in the air. Warm air can hold more water vapour than cold air before reaching saturation. Above the saturation point, any further moisture or any further cooling causes condensation — water vapour turns into liquid droplets that we see as cloud, fog, mist or rain.

The dew point is the temperature to which a given air parcel must be cooled at constant pressure to reach saturation. Below the dew point, water condenses. A worked example: an air mass at 17 °C containing 5 g of water vapour per cubic metre is relatively dry. If that same air mass is cooled to about -7 °C, the same 5 g/m³ now corresponds to 100 % humidity — saturation. Further cooling forms cloud droplets.

sections.caption: Humidity, saturation and the dew point of an air parcel.

sections.text: Clouds form when an air parcel is cooled below its dew point. The most common mechanism is mechanical lifting: thermal convection from a heated ground, orographic lift by terrain, or warm air rising over colder air at a front. With higher humidity, condensation occurs at lower altitudes — so very humid air tends to give many low clouds and a low cloud base.
Moist adiabatic lapse rate

A dry air parcel rising in the atmosphere cools by the dry adiabatic lapse rate of 1 °C per 100 m. Once the parcel cools to its dew point, condensation begins inside it. The phase change releases latent heat into the parcel, which partially counteracts the adiabatic cooling. As a result, saturated rising air cools more slowly than dry rising air.

sections.caption: Moist adiabatic — slower cooling thanks to latent heat release.

sections.text: The moist (or wet) adiabatic lapse rate is therefore less than 1 °C / 100 m — typically about 0.5 to 0.6 °C per 100 m. The exact value depends on how much water condenses per metre of rise, which in turn depends on the temperature and humidity of the parcel. Warm, very humid air condenses the most water and therefore cools the most slowly.

The same principle works in reverse on the way down. Saturated air sinking and warming evaporates some of its liquid water, which absorbs heat — so warming on the descent is also slower than the 1 °C / 100 m dry rate. This is part of the engine of Föhn: moist air ascends slowly cooled, condenses and drops its water on the windward side, then descends fast-warming dry air on the lee side.

sections.title: Water in the air
items: Condensation, freezing, deposition → release heat.
Evaporation, melting, sublimation → absorb heat.
Dew point = the temperature at which an air parcel is just saturated.
Moist adiabatic ~0.5–0.6 °C / 100 m, less than dry.

chapters.title: Clouds, fog and mist
chapters.subtitle: Reading the sky
chapters.intro: Every cloud type tells a story about what the atmosphere is doing. From friendly cumulus to towering cumulonimbus, the picture in the sky is the first weather report a pilot reads.

sections.text: Fog, mist, cloud — same physics

A cloud is a portion of the atmosphere containing countless tiny droplets of liquid water suspended in the air. Fog and cloud are physically the same thing — fog is just a cloud viewed from the inside. More precisely, fog is defined as a cloud reducing horizontal visibility below 1 km. Mist is a thinner suspension of droplets and particles, defined when visibility is between 1 and 10 km.

Cloud forms whenever air is cooled below its dew point. Two main mechanisms produce that cooling. The first is radiation cooling: during long autumn and winter nights the ground emits infrared radiation and gradually cools, the air above it cools too, and if it reaches its dew point, radiation fog forms near the ground. The second is the cooling that comes from rising air, with three main causes: convection (heating from below driving thermals), orographic lift (wind forced upward by terrain like a dam) and frontal lift (warm air sliding up over colder air at a front).

sections.caption: How cumulus clouds form — thermals rise, reach dew point, condense.
Cloud base — the altitude at which condensation begins.

sections.text: Cloud names

The Latin naming system uses prefixes for altitude and suffixes for structure. Cirro- means very high cloud, between 6000 and 10000 m, generally thin. Alto- means mid-level cloud, between 3000 and 6000 m. No prefix means low cloud, below 3000 m. The suffixes describe shape and behaviour. Cumulus means ball- or cauliflower-shaped heaped cloud. Stratus means stretched layered cloud. Nimbo- (or -nimbus) means precipitating cloud.

sections.caption: Cloud overview — a family portrait of the main types.
Cloud naming — the 2×3 grid of altitude and shape.
Cloud names and the families they belong to.
Cloud names — second page of the visual catalogue.

sections.text: High clouds — cirrus and cirrocumulus

Cirrus (Ci) are small thin hair-shaped high clouds, between 6000 and 10000 m. Cirrostratus (Cs) is a thin translucent layer at the same altitudes; sunlight passes through with a halo of colours around the Sun. Cirrocumulus (Cc) are small piled-up clouds at the top of the cumulus family, with bases above 6000 m, always made of ice crystals suspended like dust.
Cumulus family — the pilot's friend and foe

Cumulus (Cu) is the classic sign of good thermals. There are three sizes. Cumulus humilis (very small, flat-bottomed) and cumulus mediocris (medium, cauliflower shape) are the indicators of an active thermal day. Their bases lie typically between 1000 and 4000 m depending on humidity and location. Cumulus congestus is much taller and darker-based; it can produce small local showers or grow further into a cumulonimbus.

Cumulonimbus (Cb) is the thunderstorm cloud — cumulus congestus that has grown into a huge mushroom-shaped tower reaching up to about 10000 m. The internal updrafts, downdrafts and horizontal currents are much stronger than any paraglider can fly; precipitation, hail and lightning are common. Strong downdrafts during the precipitation phase are caused by cooling from evaporation. A grey curtain of vertical stripes extending from the cloud base to the ground is the visible sign that precipitation is reaching the surface.

sections.caption: Thermals seen as a cumulus picture in the sky.
Cloud coverage — reading the sky for the day's potential.

sections.text: Mid-level cloud warning signs

Altocumulus castellanus (Ac cas) are tower-shaped clouds with castle-like ramparts that appear early in the morning. They signal unstable, humid air aloft and commonly predict thunderstorm development by mid-afternoon. Lenticular altocumulus (Ac len) are smooth lens-shaped clouds that develop above 3500 m when moderately strong winds flow over mountains, creating standing waves. Despite the wind, the cloud stays stationary because air condenses on the upwind side of the wave and evaporates on the downwind side.

Altostratus (As) is a mid-level cloud layer with its base around 4000 m, often heralding an approaching warm front. Altocumulus (Ac) is similar but in piled patches at about 4500 m. Stratus (St) is a low cloud layer often touching the ground — fog from above. Stratus is typical of autumn and winter mornings with a strong inversion in the 1000–1500 m range: sunny at altitude, foggy below. Stratocumulus (Sc) is low banded cumulus common after rain. Nimbostratus (Ns) is a very thick low cloud layer producing extended steady precipitation, base around 1000 m.

sections.caption: Cloud naming grid — altitudes and shapes side by side.

sections.title: Cloud cheat-sheet
items: Fog = visibility < 1 km. Mist = 1–10 km.
Cirro- > 6000 m; alto- 3000–6000 m; low cloud < 3000 m.
Cumulus humilis/mediocris = active thermals.
Cumulonimbus, congestus growing, lenticularis, altocu castellanus → land or do not fly.

chapters.title: Pressure systems and prevailing winds
chapters.subtitle: Isobars, the Coriolis effect, highs and lows
chapters.intro: Wind is air rushing from high pressure to low pressure — but not in a straight line. The Earth's rotation bends it, isobars chart it and pilots read it on every flying day.

sections.text: Measuring wind

Wind direction is conventionally given as the compass direction from which the wind is blowing — a north wind blows from north toward south. Directions are stated in degrees from 0 (north) clockwise through 90 (east), 180 (south) and 270 (west). Wind speed is measured by an anemometer and reported in km/h, m/s or knots. A useful conversion: 1 knot ≈ 1.85 km/h, so 10 knots is about 18 km/h.

sections.caption: Wind direction — given as the direction the wind is FROM.
Wind strength — conversion between knots, m/s and km/h.

sections.text: Why pressure varies — and how to compare

Not all locations are at sea level, so the pressure measured locally cannot be compared directly: the highest station always reads the lowest pressure. Meteorologists therefore correct every reading to its sea-level equivalent before plotting it on a map. Even at sea level, atmospheric pressure varies from place to place and from one moment to another, because changes in land temperature warm or cool the air in contact with the ground, which then varies in density and pressure.

An area of high pressure tends to form over land that is cooler than its surroundings for a long period — the Arctic, Siberia, any continent in winter, or an ocean during summer that is cooler than the neighbouring continents. Conversely, a low pressure area (depression) forms over a region warmer than its surroundings — the equator, the Sahara in summer, or the Atlantic in winter. These persistent highs and lows are called centres of action because they drive the general atmospheric circulation.
Isobars and typical pressures

An isobar on a weather map is a line connecting points of equal sea-level-corrected pressure. Isobars are typically drawn every 5 hPa. The average sea-level pressure on land is around 1015 hPa. A strong winter anticyclone can reach 1030 to 1040 hPa; higher values are very rare. Summer highs are generally lower, around 1020 to 1025 hPa. A pressure of 955 hPa would be typical of a deep depression.

sections.caption: Reading an isobar chart — pressure contours and gradient.
Highs and lows on an isobar chart.
Reading an isobar chart in detail.

sections.text: Closely packed isobars mean a steep horizontal pressure gradient and strong winds. Widely spaced isobars mean a weak gradient and light winds. The strength of the wind at a given place can be read directly off the chart by looking at how tightly the contours are packed near that place.

sections.caption: Wind strength — tighter isobars mean stronger gradient wind.
Strength of high and low pressure systems.

sections.text: Coriolis and geostrophic wind

Because the Earth rotates, moving air in the northern hemisphere is deflected to the right of its direction of motion. This interferes with the direct movement of air between centres of action and gives the wind a rotational character. Wind rotates clockwise around a high (anticyclone) and anti-clockwise around a low (cyclone) in the northern hemisphere. A memory aid: keep 'anti-' in the middle — anti-cyclone clockwise, cyclone anti-clockwise. In the southern hemisphere it is reversed.

sections.caption: Rotation imposed by the Coriolis force.
Coriolis on a real chart example.

sections.text: High in the atmosphere, well above the friction of the ground, wind blows parallel to the isobars — this is the geostrophic wind. Three principles describe it. Wind direction is parallel to the isobars. Wind rotates clockwise around a high, anti-clockwise around a low (northern hemisphere). Wind strength is determined by isobar spacing. Nearer the surface, friction slows the wind and tilts it slightly toward the low — typically a few degrees over flat ground.

sections.caption: Wind from pressure equalisation — between high and low.
Wind profile over the ground — friction slows the lowest layers.

sections.text: Anticyclones vs depressions

An anticyclone (high) is a region where the air falls, heats by compression and dries out. In Europe there is often a temperature inversion accompanying a high pressure region, typically between 1500 and 2000 m. Anticyclonic weather is therefore rather stable with a convective layer of modest depth. A depression (cyclone) is a region of rising air: the air cools, increases its humidity and the weather conditions are unsettled, often cloudy or rainy.

Between these two extremes is the 'barometric swamp' — a wide region of intermediate pressure (1010 to 1020 hPa) with widely spaced isobars. Air density is lower, the atmosphere is more unstable, more humid and more volatile than in an anticyclone, while winds are light because the pressure gradient is small or zero. The barometric swamp is one of the best paragliding situations, with good convection and high thermal ceilings — but the risk of local afternoon thunderstorms is real.

sections.caption: Formation of polar and tropical air masses & pressure systems.
Formation of thermal high and low pressure.
How wind forms — pressure equalisation in action.

sections.title: Pressure essentials
items: Isobars close together → strong wind.
Northern hemisphere: clockwise round highs, anti-clockwise round lows.
Anticyclone: descending, stable, often inversion at 1500–2000 m.
Depression: rising air, humid, unsettled.
Barometric swamp (1010–1020 hPa, light winds): often the best PG day, but local Cb risk.

chapters.title: Local winds and Föhn
chapters.subtitle: Valley wind, mountain wind, alpine pumping, Föhn
chapters.intro: Inside the Alps, local thermal circulations and Föhn often dominate over the synoptic wind. Learning their daily rhythm and danger signs is essential for safe flying. Together with thunderstorms, Föhn is the deadliest weather phenomenon for Swiss paraglider pilots.

sections.text: Valley wind (anabatic)

Beyond the general geostrophic circulation, regional air movements develop on a mesoscale and microscale. The classic alpine example is the alternation of valley wind by day and mountain wind by night. The valley wind blows from the main, wide section of a valley up toward its upper reaches with their small side valleys and peaks, which warm up faster than the broader main valley.

The valley wind is driven by solar intensity and is enhanced by snow-free ground. It is strongest in July and August and weakest in December and January. The daily cycle: the wind begins around 11 h, peaks in mid-afternoon (~15 h), and the opposite mountain wind takes over around 18-19 h after sunset. With altitude the valley wind strengthens, but in wide valleys it gradually disappears around 2000 m, where the prevailing synoptic wind takes over.

In Switzerland the main alpine valleys — Rhone, Rhine, Reuss — channel by far the strongest valley winds because they collect the flow from many tributary valleys like the trunk of a tree. In spring, with snow-free pre-alps but still-cold inner-alpine air, valley winds in the Rhone valley routinely reach 50-60 km/h at the valley floor by mid-afternoon. A pilot thermalling above the valley must plan the descent carefully: it is often safer to top-land than to risk a high-wind valley landing. Local 'spillover' phenomena (e.g. at the Maloja or Grimsel pass) produce additional turbulent down-flows on the lee side of passes and require local knowledge.

sections.caption: Valley wind — anabatic flow up sun-warmed slopes.
Alpine pumping — the daily heartbeat of valley and mountain wind.
Eastern Switzerland — regional winds in detail.

sections.text: Mountain wind (katabatic)

After sunset each valley wall is cooled by nocturnal radiation. Cold, dense air slides down the slopes and accumulates at the bottom of the valley, then flows down-valley toward the broader and deeper main valley which cools more slowly. This is the katabatic mountain wind. It is generally lighter than the daytime valley wind, beginning typically around 18-19 h in summer, but can build into surprisingly strong drainage flows on long deep valleys. Pilots launching late in the day in deep Alpine valleys must factor in the moment the mountain wind 'switches on' — sometimes it becomes impossible to fly out from a take-off site once the drainage flow sets in.

sections.caption: Mountain wind — cold air draining downslope after sunset.

sections.text: Sometimes the convergence of evening downslope breezes from both sides of a valley generates a slight upward current in the middle, called a reverse thermal or convergence updraft. It can be just strong enough to keep a glider aloft for tens of minutes after sunset — a classic example is the late-evening lift above Interlaken. This is locally known as 'rendition' lift.
Föhn — the ingredients

Föhn is a warm, dry, gusty downslope wind. The classic ingredients are two: a strong pressure difference across the mountain chain, and a barrier tall enough to force the air to release moisture as it rises. The same phenomenon exists wherever big mountains and a pressure gradient coincide — called Chinook in the Rockies, Zonda in the Andes, and many other regional names — but the dynamics are identical. In Switzerland the Alps make the Föhn a daily talking point in winter, spring and autumn.

On the windward side the moist air ascends, condenses and drops its water as rain — cooling at the slow moist adiabatic rate of about 0.6 °C / 100 m. The cloud clings to the ridge and forms a compact band at the crest called the Föhn wall. Föhn does NOT always produce a wall cloud or rain on the upwind side: a 'dry Föhn' can occur under a clear blue sky and is just as turbulent. A Föhn 'knee' (Föhnknie) in the isobars over the Alpine main ridge on the synoptic chart is another tell-tale.

sections.caption: Föhn — warm, dry, gusty wind on the lee of an alpine chain.
Föhn diagram — moist ascent, dry descent.

sections.text: On the lee side the now-dry air descends, warming at the fast dry adiabatic rate of 1 °C / 100 m. Because moisture has been removed on the way up, the descending air arrives much warmer and drier than the air that started on the windward side. It is also strong and gusty, with violent turbulence. The Föhn 'wedges' down into the lee-side valleys like a vacuum sucking in air, and can produce extremely strong winds at the valley floor even when the launch above seems calm. The conclusion is simple: if the Föhn is active, do not fly, even if 'the weather looks good'.
Südföhn and Nordföhn

Südföhn blows from the south across the Alps. It is driven by a depression over northwest Europe and higher pressure over northern Italy. On the ground there is a strong south-north pressure difference (south higher) — for example 1010 hPa at Zurich and 1019 hPa at Lugano. The effect is clouds and heavy rain on the southern slopes, strong winds, high temperatures and sunny dry conditions north of the Alps. Risk is concentrated in the northern Alpine valleys where the fine weather may tempt the pilot to fly. Südföhn very often develops just ahead of an approaching westerly cold front — but not always.

Nordföhn is the mirror image: an Azores-area anticyclone extends north and east, often with a depression over Eastern Europe. The ground pressure difference reverses: 1023 hPa Zurich and 1015 hPa Lugano (north higher). Clouds and rain accumulate on the north side, while warm dry sunny weather and high winds develop on the south side, especially in Ticino. Risk is again on the sunny side where flying looks deceptively safe.

sections.caption: Föhnknie — the characteristic 'knee' in the wind pattern.
Föhn regions — typical alpine corridors affected.

sections.text: Reading the signs

Föhn produces strong turbulence, rotor downwind of the mountains and rapid changes in wind direction. Visible warning signs: the Föhn wall (a stationary cloud bank piled on the windward side of the ridge), lenticular clouds in the lee (smooth lens shapes that stay still while the wind howls through them), sudden clarity of the air at altitude, rapidly rising temperature in the lee valleys, and on the synoptic chart a sharp 'knee' in the isobars over the Alpine ridge.

The practical Swiss threshold is a north-south pressure difference of 4 hPa between Zurich and Lugano. Although some exam material quotes 5 hPa, the operational rule used by the SHV/FSVL and by experienced instructors is 4 hPa: at 4 hPa the Föhn already breaks through reliably. 8 hPa is the absolute outer limit at which only highly experienced local pilots will consider flying in specific Föhn-sheltered areas — and even there 'doors slam down' unpredictably. The forecast diagrams (MeteoSwiss Föhn diagram, SHV Föhn page) plot this difference: a curve above the 4 hPa line means Südföhn, below means Nordföhn.

sections.caption: Föhn — cloud pictures that announce it.

sections.title: Local winds checklist
items: Valley wind: anabatic, starts ~11 h, peaks ~15 h, July–August. Up to 50–60 km/h in Rhone/Rhine/Reuss valleys in spring.
Mountain wind: katabatic, starts ~18–19 h after sunset.
Föhn needs strong pressure difference + tall barrier (moisture on windward side is helpful but not mandatory — 'dry Föhn' exists).
Föhn signs: wall cloud on windward side, lenticularis on lee, sudden warming and clarity, Föhnknie in isobars.
Δp Zurich–Lugano ≥ 4 hPa: Föhn likely — DO NOT FLY. 8 hPa is the absolute outer expert-only limit.
Lee thermals are stronger but bumpier than windward thermals — the prevailing wind shreds the column.

chapters.title: Turbulence
chapters.subtitle: Mechanical, shear and thermal
chapters.intro: Almost every paragliding incident has turbulence somewhere in its causal chain. Knowing the four main types — and where to expect each — is the difference between a routine flight and a frightening one.

sections.text: What turbulence is

Turbulence is the local variation, eddying and abrupt change in wind direction and speed that the atmosphere produces when smooth flow is disturbed. It is fundamentally driven by wind: the stronger the wind, the greater the chance of turbulence and the more violent it can be. Flying in strong winds, or in areas prone to strong winds (a narrowing or a curve of a valley), is therefore unwise. Storms are particularly bad because they are often associated with episodes of strong, irregular gusts; pilots should land quickly when storms approach.

sections.caption: Turbulence — air motion irregular in speed and direction.

sections.text: Meteorologists classify turbulence into three types according to origin: mechanical turbulence (wind meeting solid obstacles), shear turbulence (between layers of different wind speed or direction) and thermal turbulence (between warm rising and cool descending air).
Mechanical turbulence

Mechanical turbulence is the result of conflict between solid obstacles and wind. Objects upwind of a reference point are described as 'windward'; objects downwind are 'leeward'.
