The air leeward of an obstacle has the most marked turbulence — rotor flow that may extend horizontally up to ten times the obstacle's height downwind. It is therefore dangerous to fly leeward of a mountain, a building or any tall obstacle, and this should be avoided at all costs, including in the choice of landing sites.

Where wind is forced over a relief, there is a constriction in the airflow above and just behind the edge, and the air is forced to increase its speed. This phenomenon of increased air speed where the flow area decreases is the Venturi effect. The Venturi effect does not only occur above and behind mountain peaks but also wherever wind is forced into a narrow valley or around a ridge — a strong contributor to turbulence around mountain passes and gaps.

sections.caption: Venturi effect — wind accelerates through narrow gaps.

sections.text: Shear turbulence

Shear turbulence occurs close to the boundary between two air masses subjected to winds of different speed or direction. A typical example: a 10–15 knot north-easterly wind at 1000 and 2000 m, with a southwesterly 10 knots at 3000 and 4000 m — between 2000 and 3000 m there is a sharp change of direction that produces shear turbulence at the boundary.

When wind speed alone decreases without changing direction, the result is a 'gradient wind' — most marked near a flat surface where the wind a few centimetres above the ground is hampered by friction while the wind tens of metres above is much stronger. The wind gradient near the ground can therefore be a source of significant shear turbulence on take-off and landing.
Thermal turbulence

Thermal turbulence occurs at the boundary between warm, rising air and cool, descending air. It is also strong at the top of the convective layer, where rising thermals meet stable air aloft. Thermal turbulence is therefore strongest at the times and places where convection is most active — typically mid-afternoon on sunny days, where sun and shadow alternate, and in valleys where the valley wind meets thermals from south-facing slopes.

In a typical alpine summer afternoon you may meet all three kinds of turbulence in a single hour. Near the ground (within 20 m): mainly mechanical from terrain and vegetation. Around 500 m above a valley with a developed valley wind: shear turbulence between the valley wind and the prevailing wind aloft. Above a south slope at 2800 m on a sunny day: thermal turbulence dominates.

sections.title: Turbulence types
items: Mechanical: wind + obstacles → rotors downwind, up to 10× obstacle height.
Shear: layers with different wind speed/direction → boundary turbulence.
Thermal: rising bubbles → gusts in convective sky.
Venturi: wind accelerated through narrow gaps.

chapters.title: Air masses and fronts
chapters.subtitle: Cold front, warm front, occlusion
chapters.intro: Big weather changes come from the meeting of dissimilar air masses. The interface between them — the front — produces the characteristic cloud sequences and wind shifts every pilot has to recognise. Cold fronts are among the three top dangers for paraglider pilots (alongside Föhn and valley wind).

sections.text: Air masses

An air mass is an extensive body of air — a continental scale of hundreds or thousands of kilometres — with relatively uniform characteristics, especially temperature and humidity, extending horizontally to its boundaries. Examples are a polar maritime air mass (cold and wet) or a tropical continental one (hot and dry). An air mass is described as 'warm' when its temperature is higher than that of its neighbours, regardless of what an absolute thermometer says — two air masses at -20 °C and -10 °C define a 'warm' and 'cold' pair just as validly as +5 °C and +20 °C.

Air masses do not mix easily. There is a relatively clear separation between two distinct air masses, called a front. Fronts are found mainly in temperate regions like Europe, where cold polar and warm tropical air masses meet. In polar, equatorial or tropical regions, fronts are essentially absent because all the air is of the same character.

sections.caption: Formation of polar and tropical air masses.
Polar and tropical air mass formation — global picture.
Boundary between two air masses — sloped, not vertical.
Boundary between air masses — a closer look.

sections.text: How fronts form in a depression

At our latitudes, depressions and frontal systems usually move from west to east. The anti-clockwise rotation of winds around a depression, combined with the eastern trajectory of the depression itself, drives the cold air west of the depression toward the southeast — forming a cold front — while warm air on the east side heads north, forming a warm front. Both fronts slant toward the cold air. The cold front moves about twice as fast as the warm front, so over time the cold front catches up and the system 'closes' into an occlusion.

sections.caption: How fronts form — cyclone rotation drives both fronts.
Cyclonic influence — depressions drive the front system.
Front formation under a cyclone — close-up.

sections.text: Warm front

A warm front occurs when warm, less dense air moves over the top of retreating cold air, sliding gently up the slope. Because the slope is gentle and the air mass is slow, an observer on the ground first sees the warm front high overhead, then watches it descend as the front passes. Cool air rises by expansion, water vapour saturates, clouds form and rain falls. A warm front advances at roughly 15-25 km/h and takes 12-24 hours (sometimes more) to cross Switzerland — it is a long, slow event, sometimes spanning two days of poor flying.

The cloud sequence in front of a warm front is a textbook display: high cirrus first, then cirrostratus and altostratus, then nimbostratus with steady rain at the front itself. Just before the warm front arrives on the ground, clouds are low and produce rain, visibility is poor and the wind strength increases. The whole sequence can stretch over hundreds of kilometres; precipitation lasts for hours. Embedded thunderstorms hidden behind the warm-front cloud layer (especially with an approaching occlusion) are a particular danger because they are invisible from below.

sections.caption: Warm front — gentle slope, long approach with high cirrus first.
Cloud images that announce a warm front.

sections.text: Cold front

A cold front occurs when cold, denser air slides violently under warm air it encounters. The boundary is much steeper than for a warm front; the warm air is shoved abruptly upward. Cloud is shorter in horizontal extent but much taller. The front is characterised by a barrier of cumulonimbus coming from the west, often with thunderstorms, sharp gusts and rapid temperature drop. A cold front advances at 40-70 km/h and crosses Switzerland in just 6-12 hours — about twice as fast as a warm front.

The hours before a cold front can be calm and even sunny, sometimes preceded in Switzerland by Südföhn. Then the front and its barrier of cumulonimbus arrive, sometimes with lightning, thunder and hail. Behind the cold front the trailing air is fresher, with good visibility and stratocumulus or shallow cumulus at modest altitude. Cumulonimbus and thunderstorms are more likely in summer cold fronts than winter ones, because the lower atmosphere is warmer. Meteorological forecasts that mention 'fresh polar air arriving in a hot stuffy summer' usually mean a cold-front passage with thunderstorms, hail and gusty winds.

A particular danger sign on the weather radar is a 'squall line' — a row of cumulonimbus cells aligned along the cold front. A visible squall line on the radar demands an immediate landing decision even if the line is still 100 km away, because the cold-air outflow ahead of the storms can produce destructive ground-level winds far in advance of the visible precipitation. Always check both the radar and the sky: radar updates lag by 15-30 minutes.

sections.caption: Cold front — steep slope, towering convective cloud.
Cold front cloud sequence.
Active front vs. just a brush — how to tell.

sections.text: Occlusion

The cold front moves about twice as fast as the warm front. Eventually the cold front catches up with the warm front and the two merge — this is called an occlusion. Depending on the relative temperature of the cold air behind the cold front and the cold air ahead of the warm front, an occlusion presents either warm-front or cold-front characteristics at ground level.

If the cold air behind the cold front is warmer than the cold air ahead of the warm front, the cold front slides over the warm front and the conditions at ground level remain warm-front in character. If the situation is reversed and the trailing cold air is colder, the cold front slips under the warm front and the occlusion presents cold-front character at ground level. The practical danger of an occlusion is 'masked' thunderstorms: cumulonimbus towers can be embedded behind the warm-front cloud layer, invisible to the pilot under the overcast yet still producing the usual outflow and gusts when they pass overhead.

sections.caption: Formation of an occlusion — cold front overtakes the warm front.
Well-developed occlusion — composite weather.
Occlusion — combined warm+cold front character.
Occlusion — cloud images of the merged front.
Polar front / stationary front — when fronts don't move.

sections.title: Front facts
items: Warm front: gentle slope, high cirrus first, long steady rain. 15–25 km/h, 12–24 h across Switzerland.
Cold front: steep slope, towering Cb, brief violent passage. 40–70 km/h, 6–12 h across Switzerland — twice as fast as a warm front.
Squall-line radar signature ahead of a cold front: land immediately, even if the line is 100 km away.
Cold fronts move faster than warm fronts and can overtake them to form an OCCLUSION (mixed front type).
Occlusion danger: 'masked' thunderstorms hidden behind the warm-front cloud deck.

chapters.title: Heat, storms and Swiss weather
chapters.subtitle: Cumulonimbus, synoptic maps and typical situations
chapters.intro: Heat storms and frontal storms have different drivers and different timings — and Switzerland's geography produces a small catalogue of repeating weather patterns that every alpine pilot learns to recognise. Thunderstorms are the single greatest hazard in paragliding, and the gust-front outflow they produce is the most underestimated of all weather dangers.

sections.text: Heat storms

For strong thermals to develop over a land surface, two ingredients are needed. First, strong solar radiation arriving essentially perpendicular to the surface — for instance mountain faces in the morning, southern slopes around noon, western slopes in the afternoon. Second, ground that absorbs solar radiation efficiently: dark, rough, dry surfaces warm the air well; reflective surfaces such as snow waste much of the incoming light; wet ground spends a large fraction of the energy on evaporation.

Powerful convection in a humid atmosphere can build cumulus humilis through mediocris into a cumulus congestus and then a cumulonimbus producing rain, gusts and thunder — a heat storm. Heat storms usually develop in late afternoon, when the ground has had hours of sun to fuel convection. They are local, often isolated, but extremely dangerous within tens of kilometres because of strong inflow, downdrafts and gust fronts.

sections.caption: Thunderstorm formation — congestus building into Cb.
Thunderstorm formation — second stage of growth.
Thunderstorms — mature and decaying stages.
Thunderstorm hazards — gusts, hail, lightning.

sections.text: Warning signs in the sky

The development of a thunderstorm is almost always visible in advance if you know what to look for. Watch the cumulus field. Early in the day small puffy cumulus form one by one over thermal triggers — these are friends. As the day matures: (1) the bases of neighbouring clouds start to merge into one large dark base spanning several kilometres; (2) the cloud base above darkens because the tower has grown tall enough to block the sun from below; (3) suddenly 'everything starts lifting' — instead of localised thermal cores, the whole valley produces broad lift, signalling that the storm's organised inflow has begun. By this point the door is closing fast.

If a grey curtain of precipitation is visible from the cloud base toward the ground, the storm is fully developed. Land immediately; ideally outside the storm's anvil region. Cumulonimbus can grow all the way to the tropopause (~10000 m) where the stratosphere blocks them and they spread sideways into the classic anvil.

Frontal thunderstorms are not tied to time of day. They are triggered by the violent uplift of warm humid air at a cold front and can occur day or night. A 'blue thermal' is convection that does not result in cumulus at the top — it occurs when the air is very dry (low humidity or a wide temperature/dew-point spread), so condensation does not happen at the thermal top. Convective activity in Europe is best from April to August because solar radiation is stronger and the days are longer; the snowmelt shifts the peak from May in the pre-Alps to August in the high Alps.
The gust front — the underestimated killer

Most pilots fear being sucked up under a thunderstorm. The greater danger is being hit by its cold-air outflow many kilometres away. As the storm matures, cold air sinks from the upper part of the cloud (where condensation has chilled it) like a bucket of water being tipped over, accelerated by evaporative cooling in the precipitation shaft. This dense cold air spreads horizontally along the ground in all directions, producing strong, gusty winds — a gust front — that can run 20–50 km ahead of the visible storm.

The gust front catches pilots in air that looks clear and calm. Landings under a developing gust front are extremely turbulent and have killed experienced pilots. A gust-front outflow from one storm can also trigger the next storm by lifting warm valley air, in a self-sustaining chain reaction. Practical rule: if any cumulonimbus is on the radar within ~50 km, land. A squall line of multiple cells along a cold front is a categorical no-fly signature.
Synoptic maps

A synoptic chart summarises the state of various meteorological features at a given time — pressure as isobars, fronts as marked lines, and sometimes wind, temperature and cloud. The letter H marks the centre of a high (anticyclone), the letter L the centre of a low (depression). The line styles distinguish warm fronts, cold fronts and occlusions. Pressures shown on the map are corrected to sea level.

sections.caption: Fronts on the weather chart — symbols and movement.
Fronts — schematic of warm, cold and occluded boundaries.

sections.text: Three things on a synoptic map answer most flying questions. Isobar spacing indicates the pressure gradient and therefore the wind strength. Centres of action (H and L) determine the wind rotation: clockwise round a high, anti-clockwise round a low (northern hemisphere). The position and movement of fronts determine the next 24 hours of weather. A 'Föhnknie' — a sharp kink in the isobars over the Alpine main ridge — signals an active Föhn flow.
Typical Swiss situations

From the synoptic maps over Europe we can identify six weather patterns typical for Switzerland. An anticyclone centred on the Alps gives stable weather: in winter, pressures of 1030-1040 hPa, often with strong inversion and stratus on the plateau; in summer, around 1020-1025 hPa, with usable thermals in the Alps and possible local thunderstorms. A barometric plateau gives light winds and good thermals but a real thunderstorm risk in the afternoon.

The Bise is a pronounced wind from the north-east on the central plateau, caused by a high-pressure centre north of Germany. On the Plateau a Bise typically blows 30-50 km/h; in strong events high fog (Hochnebel) can be pushed up to 1500-1800 m, leaving the Plateau under a thick stratus deck while the Alps stay sunny above. Westerly flow comes with a depression over Scandinavia: short periods of good weather alternate with disturbances, often with strong winds. The two Föhn situations — Südföhn (depression northwest, high over northern Italy, with south wind across the Alps) and Nordföhn (Azores high extending east, with north wind across the Alps) — are characterised by strong south-north pressure differences and dangerous turbulence.

The practical Swiss Föhn threshold is a Zurich-Lugano pressure difference of 4 hPa, not 5 hPa. At 4 hPa Föhn already breaks through reliably; 8 hPa is the outer expert-only limit. The MeteoSwiss and SHV Föhn diagrams plot exactly this difference: a curve above 4 hPa indicates Südföhn, below it indicates Nordföhn.

Sunny weather at altitude with fog in the valleys is a classic high-pressure morning: an anticyclone with descending air, an inversion in the lower layers and pooled cool moist air below. Simultaneous sunshine at altitude and in the valleys is the signature of a stable, well-developed anticyclone.

Thermal high and low pressure formation.

items: Heat storms peak in the afternoon; frontal storms can come any time.
Watch the sky: merging cumulus bases, darkening base, 'everything lifting at once' = the storm door is closing.
Gust front outflow can reach 20–50 km ahead of the visible storm — the most underestimated thunderstorm danger.
Squall line on radar = no-fly, even if 100 km away.
Synoptic map → highs, lows, fronts and pressure gradient.
Δp Zurich–Lugano ≥ 4 hPa → Föhn (NOT 5 hPa). 8 hPa is the expert-only outer limit.

books.title: Law and Airspace
books.subtitle: OACS, airspace classes, glider chart, VFR rules and licensing.

chapters.title: Legal framework
chapters.subtitle: OACS, OFAC/BAZL, insurance and identification
chapters.intro: Free flight in Switzerland is governed by a small set of federal regulations. Knowing what they require — and what you must carry on every flight — is your legal foundation.

sections.text: Who makes the rules

In Switzerland only the Confederation has the authority to legislate on the use of the airspace. The Federal Office of Civil Aviation (FOCA / OFAC / BAZL) is the federal agency that implements and enforces these rules. The OACS (Ordinance on Aircraft of Special Categories), in force since 1994, is the specific text that covers hang gliders and paragliders.

sections.caption: The authorities and regulations behind paragliding in Switzerland.
Legal basics for hang gliders and paragliders.

sections.text: Insurance

Liability insurance covering at least CHF 1,000,000 for third-party damage on the ground is mandatory. Damage to other aircraft in flight is not covered by this minimum: separate cover is required if you want it. The insurance certificate number must match the wing's identification number.
Identification on the wing

Every slope glider must carry two sets of markings: an identification number and a manufacturer's plaque. The identification number is composed of five or more digits, must be easily recognisable, and is affixed to the underside of the wing. The digits are usually adhered to the fabric and each must measure 40 cm in height. The SHV/FSVL manages and allocates these numbers; in practice the number is usually the same as the pilot's SHV/FSVL licence number.

The manufacturer's plaque is a small piece of fabric sewn into an edge or wingtip and must include the manufacturer's name, the type of wing, the year of manufacture, and the minimum and maximum loads specified by the manufacturer. Other items such as serial numbers or class certification are not mandatory. All wings flown by the same pilot must carry the same identification number — identical to the number on the certificate of liability insurance, which the pilot must carry on every flight.

sections.caption: Hang glider / paraglider as defined in Swiss law.

sections.text: What you must carry in flight

During every flight you must carry your insurance certificate (with a number matching the wing) and your pilot licence. Police officers, BAZL officials and airport managers are entitled to request both at any time.
Where you may not take off or land

Take-off and landing are prohibited on public roads, ski slopes, within 5 km of an airfield runway (without a CTR authorisation) and within 2.5 km of a heliport. Take-off and landing on public water surfaces are forbidden.
Tandem flights
A Tandem A licence allows any passenger. A Tandem B licence requires that the passenger holds a solo licence in the same category. Carrying more than one passenger in flight is not permitted.
Radio frequencies

Two frequencies are dedicated to free flight: 130.925 MHz for licensed air-to-air communication between hang glider and paraglider pilots, and 123.425 MHz reserved for training. Use of 130.925 MHz requires the appropriate radio authorisation.

sections.title: Carry, mark, insure
items: Mandatory liability cover ≥ CHF 1,000,000 ground damage.
40 cm ID number + manufacturer plate on every wing.
Carry licence + insurance certificate on every flight.
No take-off/landing on public roads, ski slopes, water; 5 km from runways, 2.5 km from heliports.

chapters.title: Abbreviations every pilot needs
chapters.subtitle: The language of charts and bulletins
chapters.intro: Airspace charts and bulletins are full of three- and four-letter codes. They make sense once you know what they stand for — and a few of them are exactly the abbreviations the instructor tells you to memorise before sitting the theory exam.

sections.text: Airspace abbreviations

CTR is the Control Zone, the controlled airspace around an airport's runway, extending from the ground up to a defined altitude. TMA stands for Terminal Maneuvering Area, the controlled airspace stacked above and around the CTR. AWY is an airway: a corridor of controlled airspace connecting TMAs at high altitude. FIZ is a Flight Information Zone, RMZ is a Radio Mandatory Zone, and TMZ is a Transponder Mandatory Zone.

sections.caption: Abbreviations on the glider chart.
More abbreviations — controlled and restricted zones.

sections.text: Restricted, danger and gliding zones

LS-R means a restricted area; entry is forbidden when the area is active. LS-D is a danger area: entry is technically allowed but discouraged because dangerous activities (live firing, parachute drops) may be taking place below. Confusingly, LS-R for Gliders is also marked LS-R on the chart but is OPEN to paragliders and sailplanes — it is restricted only to IFR/airline traffic. The MO suffix on an LSR-for-Gliders zone means it is active only when the military is off; the MA suffix means it is also active when the military is not released, but you can call to ask for it to be opened.
Bulletins and publications

GLDC is the Glider Chart, the Swiss VFR chart for free flight, reissued every March. AIP (Aeronautical Information Publication) is the master long-form reference, updated monthly. NOTAM (Notice to Airmen) is a daily bulletin of short-term changes — temporary airspace activations, hazards and obstacles. DABS (Daily Airspace Bulletin Switzerland), published at www.skybriefing.com, lists which airspaces are active today; it is the single most important pre-flight check. DABS times are in UTC: add 1 hour in winter and 2 hours in summer for Swiss local time.

ATIS (Automatic Terminal Information Service) is the recorded weather broadcast at an airport. The HX suffix on an airspace label means the airspace has no fixed hours — assume it can be active at any time and query its status before approaching. The activity digit 9 means the airspace is permanently active. Only Sion and Alpnach have temporary TMAs announced via the DABS.
Flight levels and identifiers

FL stands for Flight Level — altitude in hundreds of feet referenced to the standard pressure of 1013.25 hPa. One foot is 30.48 cm, so FL100 = 10000 feet = exactly 3048 m, rounded to 3050 m. To convert a Flight Level to approximate metres, multiply by 30: FL30 ~ 900 m, FL100 ~ 3050 m, FL130 ~ 3950 m, FL150 ~ 4550 m, FL195 ~ 5950 m. VFR (Visual Flight Rules) is the only mode paragliders may fly under; IFR (Instrument Flight Rules) requires an instrument-rated aircraft and pilot.

The 'LS' prefix in ICAO codes denotes Switzerland: L for Lower Europe, S for Switzerland. Every Swiss aerodrome's identifier begins with LS — LSZH = Zurich, LSGG = Geneva, LSZR = Altenrhein. AGL means Above Ground Level (used for the 600 m G ceiling), AMSL means Above Mean Sea Level (used for almost every other altitude on a chart). On the glider chart, upright numbers are AGL and italic numbers are AMSL — a small typographic distinction with very large practical consequences.

sections.title: Acronym crib
items: CTR / TMA / AWY: controlled airspaces around and between airports.
LS-R = restricted (forbidden when active). LS-D = danger (allowed, discouraged). LS-R for Gliders = green, open to paragliders.
DABS (skybriefing.com), NOTAM, AIP, GLDC: the four sources to check before flying.
FL = pressure altitude in hundreds of feet (QNE). DABS times are UTC: +1 winter, +2 summer.

chapters.title: Airspace structure
chapters.subtitle: G, E, D, C — and the controlled zones
chapters.intro: Switzerland uses four of the seven ICAO airspace classes. Knowing which class you are in, where its upper and lower limits sit, and how the limits change with time and topography is the core competence of legal flying.

sections.text: Four classes that matter

ICAO has defined seven airspace classes (A to G); in Switzerland only four apply: C, D, E and G. Airspaces E and G are uncontrolled — the only spaces a paraglider may use without clearance or radio. Airspaces C and D are controlled: C requires both clearance AND continuous radio contact, while D requires clearance but does not strictly require radio. Paragliders should not enter C or D; the only realistic situation is clipping the edge of an approach corridor on a long XC, where you may radio in and (sometimes) be granted a transit. A useful memory aid: 'Ceiling Defines Every Glide.'

sections.caption: The Swiss airspace classes — C, D, E, G.

sections.text: Airspace G — the carpet of Switzerland

Picture airspace G as a 600 m thick carpet laid over the entire topography of Switzerland. It runs from the ground up to 600 m AGL — uncontrolled, no clearance required. Because the limit is AGL, the absolute height of the G ceiling rises and falls with the terrain. Inside G, the rules below and above 300 m AGL differ in the visibility and cloud-clearance requirements — the relaxed minima apply only in the lower 300 m, not the full 600 m.
Airspace E — above G

Above G sits airspace E, the second uncontrolled space. Its upper limit varies by region. On the Jura and the Plateau (Mittelland) the limit is FL100 = 3050 m AMSL, regardless of military activity. In the Alps the limit depends on the military: 4550 m AMSL (FL150) when MIL OFF, and 3950 m AMSL (FL130) when MIL ON. The chart shows 'MIL ON' at the top when the Swiss Air Force is active.

sections.caption: Lower and upper limits of the various airspaces.
Mittelland/Jura — Alps boundary on the glider chart.

sections.text: MIL ON hours

Swiss military airspace activation (MIL ON) is Monday to Friday, 07:35-12:05 and 13:15-17:05 — the five-minute buffer is intentional, to exclude overlap with civil traffic. Holidays count as MIL OFF, so on a Federal holiday the alpine ceiling stays at 4550 m. The exact times are printed at the top of the glider chart and confirmed daily in the DABS.

sections.caption: MIL OFF activation status on the chart.
Military operating hours table.

sections.text: Over a peak — the 600 m AGL rule wins

Because G is measured AGL, the airspace ceiling rises with the terrain. Above a 4200 m alpine summit the G ceiling sits at 4800 m AMSL — above the nominal 4550 m E-to-C boundary. The exam-relevant rule: you may legally be at 4800 m above that peak as long as your ground clearance NEVER exceeds 600 m. As soon as the terrain drops away beneath you, the 4550 m limit reappears.
Controlled airspaces around airports

CTRs (Control Zones, airspace D) extend from the ground around the runway up to a defined altitude. TMAs (Terminal Maneuvering Areas) sit above the CTR as stacked boxes; their lower limit is a FIXED altitude (not a flight level) because of terrain considerations, while their upper limit floats with pressure as a flight level. AWYs (airways) connect TMAs and have an upper limit of FL195. The activity coding has three states: digit 9 = permanently active (Zurich and other large airports), HX = on demand (status must be queried), and temporary = announced via the DABS — only Sion and Alpnach in Switzerland.

There is one crucial exception for paragliders: south of the Mittelland-Alps dividing line only, you may fly UNDER a TMA up to 300 m above ground without clearance. This makes valley flying possible in places like Meiringen, where the TMA lower limit would otherwise close the airspace. The dividing line runs roughly from Montreux across central Switzerland to the Alpstein and on to Austria, and is precisely drawn on the chart.

sections.caption: CTR — Control Zone (airspace D) around an aerodrome.
TMA — Terminal Maneuvering Area above and around a CTR.
AWY — airway corridor.

sections.text: Aerodromes and heliports

Without further information, paragliders must keep 5 km from an active airfield runway (civil or military without CTR) and 2.5 km from a heliport. The protective cylinder only extends 600 m above ground, so flying over an airfield ABOVE 600 m AGL is not a violation. Local agreements (e.g. a 'wedge' cut from the cylinder at Trogen) are listed under the SHV/FSVL local agreements — worth checking when planning an XC route that grazes such a zone.
Danger, restricted and gliding zones

LS-R restricted areas (red on the chart) are forbidden when active. Frequently active ones are pre-printed on the chart, but their daily status must still be verified on the DABS. LS-D danger areas allow entry but are not recommended — live firing or other hazards may be taking place below. LS-R for Gliders zones (green outline) are restricted to IFR/airline traffic but open to paragliders and sailplanes, with REDUCED cloud-clearance minima (50 m vertical, 100 m horizontal). They are active only from 1 March to 31 October; outside this window they have no effect, and examiners exploit this in winter exams. Exclusion zones around special events (G8 Evian 2003, Davos WEF) are temporary, never permanent in Switzerland.

sections.caption: LS-R restricted areas on the chart.
LS-D danger areas — entry is permitted but risky.

G: ground to 600 m AGL everywhere — the ceiling tracks the terrain.
