chapters.title: Sources of airspace information
chapters.subtitle: Glider chart, AIP, DABS and NOTAMs
chapters.intro: Even the best memory of the airspace is no substitute for checking the live status of each zone before flying. A handful of publications cover everything you need — and one of them must be checked DAILY.

sections.text: The glider chart

The Swiss Glider Chart (GLDC) is the basic reference for free-flight airspace. It shows airspace boundaries (CTR, TMA, AWY, LS-R, LS-D), the upper and lower limits of every zone, the military operating hours, the Mittelland-Alps dividing line, the marked obstacles (towers, cables), the LS-R for Gliders soaring zones, and the activity codes on each airspace. A new edition is published every March; many pilots keep a paper copy for a few seasons for overview, then always re-check the online version at map.geo.admin.ch before each flight for the most up-to-date status.

The chart uses a fixed visual convention: green outline = LS-R for Gliders, red shading = LS-R restricted, red 'R' on its own = restricted zones, and aerodromes appear with their protective cylinder (5 km airfield, 2.5 km heliport). Upright numbers on a tag are AGL, italic numbers are AMSL — a small typographic distinction with very large consequences. The upper number on a tag is highlighted in blue when it is given as a Flight Level (standard atmosphere) rather than an absolute altitude.

sections.caption: Glider chart — map.geo.admin.ch viewer.
Anatomy of the glider chart.

sections.text: DABS — Daily Airspace Bulletin Switzerland

The DABS, at www.skybriefing.com, is the single most important pre-flight check. It is published every day and lists the temporary changes to Swiss airspace: actual MIL ON status, active LS-R restrictions, temporary TMA extensions (Sion and Alpnach), firing-range activations, parachute drops and similar. Before reading, verify that the displayed date is the day you intend to fly. Times are given in UTC — add 1 hour in winter and 2 hours in summer to get Swiss local time. A common rookie error is to look at yesterday's DABS or to forget the UTC conversion and end up flying into an active firing zone.

sections.caption: DABS — Daily Airspace Bulletin Switzerland.
DABS — example showing active firing areas.

sections.text: AIP, NOTAM and KOSIF

The AIP (Aeronautical Information Publication) is Skyguide's master reference document, describing every airspace, navaid and aerodrome in Switzerland with all general and persistent information. It is updated monthly and is the long-form source the chart summarises in visual form. NOTAMs (Notices to Airmen) are short-term operational notices about temporary changes — closures, special events, hazards and ground obstacles. KOSIF (German) / COTSENA (French), at www.skyguide.ch/kosif_f, is the daily bulletin specifically for military shooting locations and schedules.
Aerodromes, heliports and local agreements

Aerodromes and heliports are marked on the chart with their protective cylinders: 5 km from an active runway, 2.5 km from a heliport. The cylinder only extends 600 m AGL — you may legally overfly above that. Some heliports and airfields have local agreements with the SHV/FSVL that cut a 'wedge' out of the standard cylinder (Trogen is a frequently-cited example) or allow transit. Before planning an XC line that grazes such a zone, check the SHV/FSVL local-agreements page — it can turn an illegal route into a legal one.

sections.title: Pre-flight check sequence
items: Glider chart (paper or map.geo.admin.ch) — static structure.
DABS at skybriefing.com — TODAY's MIL hours and active restrictions. Verify the date! Times in UTC (+1 winter, +2 summer).
NOTAM / VFR-NOTAM — temporary closures and hazards.
KOSIF / COTSENA — military shooting.
SHV/FSVL local agreements — site-specific exceptions (e.g. Trogen).

chapters.title: VFR rules and right of way
chapters.subtitle: Visibility, cloud distances and who yields to whom
chapters.intro: Paragliders fly under Visual Flight Rules at all times. VFR defines minimum visibility, distance from cloud, and a strict priority for who has the right of way. The Swiss values are precise — and they change depending on which half of airspace G you are in.

sections.text: Visibility and cloud distances

Below 300 m AGL — i.e. only the LOWER half of airspace G, not the full 600 m — the rule is simple: 1.5 km horizontal visibility, OUTSIDE the cloud at all times, ground always in sight, and you must always be able to perform a 180-degree turn. There is no numerical cloud-clearance figure in this band: the requirement is simply to be clear of cloud and able to turn around.

sections.caption: Cloud distances in airspace G below 300 m AGL.
Cloud distances and visibility for airspace E.

sections.text: Above 300 m AGL — i.e. the upper half of G, and airspace E — the strict rule applies: 300 m vertical and 1500 m horizontal distance from any cloud at all times. Visibility must be 5 km below 3050 m AMSL and 8 km above 3050 m AMSL. The increased visibility above 3050 m exists because faster IFR traffic can emerge from cloud at high speed, leaving less time to see and avoid.
Inside an LSR for Gliders

Inside an active LS-R for Gliders zone (green outline on the chart, active 1 March to 31 October only) the cloud distances are MINIMISED to 50 m vertical and 100 m horizontal. The visibility minima (5 km below 3050 m, 8 km above) and 'ground in sight' requirement remain unchanged. Watch carefully for an exam trap: if you climb ABOVE the upper limit of an LSR-for-Gliders zone, the large cloud distances apply again — even though you started inside the reduced-distance zone, your altitude has carried you out of it.
Altimetry and Flight Levels

Below the transition altitude, altimeters are set to QNH (sea-level pressure correction) and altitudes are read in metres or feet AMSL. Above that, altimeters are set to the standard pressure 1013.25 hPa and the indication is called a Flight Level. One foot is 30.48 cm, so FL100 = 10000 ft = exactly 3048 m (rounded to 3050 m on the chart). Tag numbers on the chart that are highlighted in blue are given in standard atmosphere — they can drift up to about ±200 m from the true altitude when pressure is far from 1013 hPa. In a strong high the boundaries actually move UP (good for paragliders), in a deep low they move down (but you probably are not flying in such weather anyway).

sections.caption: Flight Levels and the standard atmosphere reference.
Effect of pressure setting on the altitude reading.

sections.text: Five questions to ask at the launch site

Before launching, the instructor recommends asking yourself five questions: (1) Season — am I between 1 March and 31 October so the LSR-for-Gliders zones are active? (2) Day and time — is MIL ON or MIL OFF, and how high may I therefore climb? (3) Side of the dividing line — am I in the Mittelland/Jura or in the Alps? (4) Altitude — where am I currently, in AGL and AMSL? (5) Location — am I inside the radius of any restricted, danger or aerodrome zone? If you can answer all five, you can answer almost any exam question.
Right of way

Right-of-way ranks aircraft by manoeuvrability: the LESS manoeuvrable wins. From highest priority to lowest: aircraft in distress > balloon > paraglider / hang glider / sailplane (all equal) > airship > towing motor aircraft > powered aircraft and helicopters. The most important practical consequence is that powered aircraft must yield to us — but only when we are flying where we belong. Asserting right-of-way over an airliner in a TMA is not a defence in court.

sections.caption: Right-of-way rules — who yields to whom.
Ridge right-of-way — the aircraft with the ridge on its right has priority.
Overtaking — pass on the right; overtaking aircraft yields.
Right-of-way ranking among aircraft types.

sections.text: Same-class converging: the aircraft on the RIGHT has priority. Head-on: both turn right. Overtaking is defined as approaching from within the rear 140-degree arc (i.e. behind the 70-degree-from-tail line on either side) — the overtaking aircraft never has priority. Overtaking is allowed in free airspace but FORBIDDEN on a slope. Spiral turns close to a slope are also forbidden. Landing: the LOWEST glider has priority. A yellow cross laid out on a field means the landing is temporarily closed.

In a thermal, the FIRST glider into the core sets the direction of turn — not the lowest or the highest. Anyone joining must follow that turn direction, because a faster wing can catch up from below and a counter-rotating encounter would close at very high relative speed. The same applies when circling with birds: turn the way they turn.

On a ridge, head-on encounters are resolved by both pilots turning AWAY from the ridge — i.e. both turn right when their orientation allows. The pilot with the slope on the LEFT has no escape route, so the pilot with the slope on the right gives way. Signal the evasion early by turning out from the slope, so the other pilot sees that you have spotted them.

sections.title: Rules to live by
items: Below 300 m AGL in G: 1.5 km visibility, OUT of cloud, ground in sight, 180-degree turn always possible.
Above 300 m AGL and in E: cloud 300 m vertical / 1500 m horizontal. Visibility 5 km below 3050 m AMSL, 8 km above.
Inside LSR for Gliders (1 Mar - 31 Oct): 50 m vertical / 100 m horizontal cloud distance. Above the zone's upper limit, the large clearances return.
Less manoeuvrable wins: balloon > paraglider/glider > airship > powered aircraft.
Same class converging: right has priority. Head-on: both turn right. Overtaking = approaching from the rear 140-degree arc; overtaken aircraft has priority.
Thermal: FIRST in sets the turn direction. Landing: LOWEST has priority. Yellow cross = closed.

chapters.title: Examination and licensing
chapters.subtitle: Becoming and remaining a Swiss free-flight pilot
chapters.intro: Switzerland keeps the path to a licence simple. Knowing the timing, structure and appeal rules of the exam protects you from surprises.

sections.text: Ages and validity

The minimum age for a Swiss free-flight licence is 16. You may start instruction from age 15. The licence has unlimited duration — no medical examination is required, and there is no minimum number of flights per year to maintain it.
Examination structure

The pilot examination has two parts: theoretical and practical. The theoretical part covers aerodynamics, meteorology, equipment, legislation and flight practice. The practical part includes a series of supervised flights with specific tasks — among them a precision landing in a 30 m diameter target circle and a 5-point pre-launch check performed in the correct order. The exam content and standards are defined by federal regulation.
Timing and re-sits

The maximum allowed interval between passing the theory and passing the practical is two years. If either part is failed, you may retake it after 30 days at the earliest. Appeal against an examiner's decision is possible: written appeal must be filed with FOCA within 30 days. The SHV/FSVL is the national body of pilots, instructors and clubs.

sections.caption: SHV / FSVL — the Swiss Hang Gliding Association.

sections.text: Wildlife respect

The OACS makes pilots responsible for respecting wildlife. The map.geo.admin.ch viewer shows wildlife reserves where flight is restricted. Pilots are expected to keep clear of golden eagle nesting areas — especially March to May, when the eagles are raising young — and to avoid chamois and ibex on south slopes in winter and spring, when their energy reserves are critical for survival. A territorial golden eagle marks its area with a characteristic undulating flight pattern; if you see one, give it space.

Beyond wildlife, the OACS sets a small number of common-sense rules that apply on every flight: do not fly over outdoor crowds, do not fly over public roads or ski slopes at low altitude, do not take off or land on water surfaces. The penalties for breaches range from fines to licence withdrawal, and most importantly any incident will be investigated against these rules.

sections.caption: map.geo.admin.ch — wildlife reserves and protected areas.
Hang gliders and wildlife — respect distance from sensitive areas.

sections.title: Examination essentials
items: Minimum age 16 (instruction from 15).
Licence has unlimited duration; no medical, no minimum flights.
Max 2 years between theory and practical pass.
Re-sit after 30 days; appeal within 30 days to FOCA.

books.title: Equipment
books.subtitle: Wing, lines, risers, harness, reserve, instruments and certification.

chapters.title: The main parts of a paraglider
chapters.subtitle: Wing, lines, risers, harness, reserve
chapters.intro: A paraglider system is more than just the canopy. Knowing the five main parts and how they connect helps you check, store and maintain your gear correctly.

sections.text: Five main parts

A complete paraglider system consists of five main parts. The wing is the canopy itself: a fabric airfoil with internal cells, ribs and Mylar reinforcements at the leading edge. The lines connect the wing to the risers — sheathed thin cords arranged in groups A (front), B, C and sometimes D (rear), plus the steering lines that run to the brake handles. The risers are strong webbing strips ending in karabiner attachment loops; they are where the lines collect together before they meet the pilot.

sections.caption: The full equipment overview — what we will cover today.
Wing components — cells, ribs, profile, edges.
Risers — A/B/C/D and the speed system.

sections.text: The harness is what the pilot wears and what attaches via two karabiners to the riser ends. It includes the seat, the back protection (foam, airbag or both), the leg and chest straps, and the container for the reserve parachute. The reserve parachute is the last piece of the system: a redundant canopy stored in or on the harness and deployed by hand if the main wing becomes uncontrollable.

Each part is engineered and certified for a particular wing model — they are not freely interchangeable. Modifying, swapping or repairing a part with non-original components changes the certification of the whole system, with consequences for both safety and insurance. The pilot's pre-flight check covers all five parts in order, every flight, every time.

sections.title: Five parts to remember
items: Wing — the canopy with its cells, ribs and profile.
Lines — connect wing to risers.
Risers — A, B, C and sometimes D, with quick links.
Harness — seat, back protection, leg straps.
Reserve — a redundant parachute.

chapters.title: The wing
chapters.subtitle: Fabric, coating, cells, ribs and what kills them
chapters.intro: The canopy is the heart of the paraglider. Its fabric, coating and internal structure are remarkable engineering — and they don't last forever.

sections.text: Fabric and coating

Wing fabric is woven polyamide (nylon) in a ripstop pattern: stronger threads run in warp and weft at regular intervals so a tear cannot propagate beyond the next barrier. A standard paraglider cloth weighs about 49 g/m^2 — for comparison, an A4 sheet of office paper is around 80 g/m^2. Lightweight hike-and-fly wings already exist at about 17 g/m^2, and that number keeps falling as fabrics improve. A coating — typically polyurethane, sometimes silicone or Mylar — fills the weave to reduce porosity, lower elasticity and improve UV resistance. A good coating can also improve mechanical strength.

sections.caption: Sailcloth — the woven, coated polyamide of a paraglider canopy.
Materials overview — fabric, coating and reinforcements.

sections.text: What porosity does to flying

As coating wears, the fabric becomes porous: air leaks through it instead of pressurising the canopy. A porous wing stalls earlier — at a higher airspeed than normal — because the cells no longer hold pressure. A parachutal stall is a flight regime in which the wing still has its shape but produces almost no horizontal motion: high sink rate, almost no airspeed.
Enemies of the wing

Coating is damaged by: detergents and cleaning agents (which directly attack the coating), humid storage, friction with sand, gravel or salt, prolonged UV and heat (a wing left in a hot car in summer is a classic ageing accelerator), seawater, cow dung and fuel. The single worst combination is a wet wing locked in a hot car or stuffed wet into its bag for weeks. Clean the wing with lukewarm water only — never with detergents. It is not damaged by extreme manoeuvres themselves, by being washed in lukewarm water, or by long cool dark storage.

sections.caption: Wing construction — what makes a modern paraglider stiff and stable.

sections.text: Cells, ribs and internal structure

The wing is divided into cells running from leading to trailing edge. Each cell has an opening at the leading edge so it can inflate; toward the wing tip (the stabilo) the openings are often closed. Between cells, vertical or oblique ribs connect the upper and lower surfaces and define the profile. More cells means a more accurate profile but more weight and folded volume.

Small vent holes called crossports are cut through the ribs so air can flow sideways inside the wing. They equalise pressure between cells, fill the closed tip cells through the open central ones, and help the wing re-inflate quickly after a partial collapse. Diagonal V-ribs (sometimes called Diagonals) carry the load from one rib to another, reducing the number of attachment points and lines needed for the same span. Highest stress on the canopy is in the front half, where lift is concentrated, and on the cells closest to the line attachment points.

Anterior cell walls near the openings are often reinforced with Mylar to keep the leading edge shape rigid even when not flying. Mylar is stiff but brittle — repeated folding bends and eventually breaks it, which is why you should never pack a wing with sharp creases at the leading edge.

Cell walls can suffer downward loads in certain manoeuvres — for instance during a B-stall, where the cells lose internal pressure and can deform negatively. Diagonal V-ribs (sometimes called Diagonals) carry load from one rib to another, reducing the number of attachment points and lines needed for the same span and the same load-distribution accuracy.

sections.title: Wing fabric facts
items: Standard cloth ~49 g/m^2; hike-and-fly cloth ~17 g/m^2 (A4 paper = 80 g/m^2).
Ripstop barriers are woven in warp AND weft so tears cannot propagate.
Crossports = holes in ribs that equalise pressure between cells, including the closed tip cells.
Mylar = stiff but brittle — never fold sharply at the leading edge.
Worst-case ageing: wet wing in a hot car or stuffed wet for weeks. Clean with lukewarm water only.

chapters.title: The lines
chapters.subtitle: Sheath, core, knots and ageing
chapters.intro: Paraglider lines are deceptively simple. The choice of core material and the way the loops are made define both the wing's performance and its life span.

sections.text: Sheath and core

A paraglider line is typically built of two parts. The sheath is the outer braided cover, made of polyester or polyamide. It carries no significant load — its job is to protect the inner core from UV and abrasion and to let manufacturers colour-separate the line groups (school wings often use red on the A group so a beginner can pick it out at a glance). The downside of a sheath is extra diameter and extra parasitic drag, so high-performance wings increasingly use unsheathed lines with a UV-resistant coating instead. The core is what actually takes the tension.

Modern cores are either oriented polyethylene (Dyneema, white) or aramid (Technora / Kevlar, light brown). Dyneema has an excellent strength-to-weight ratio, is easy to sew and resists UV well, but it stretches a little under load and slightly SHRINKS when not loaded — most noticeable in the C and D positions where each line only sees about 300-500 g in normal flight. Aramid is the opposite: dimensionally stable lengthwise, very low stretch, high melting point, but kink-sensitive and more UV-sensitive. Manufacturers therefore use a mix — typically Dyneema in the upper, lightly-loaded part of the wing and aramid in the lower, heavily-loaded sections — to keep the wing flying like new for as long as possible.

sections.caption: Line sheath material — protective outer cover.
Line core (polyethylene) — Dyneema, very low stretch.
Line core (aramid) — Kevlar, very strong but more brittle.

sections.text: Load distribution

Lift is concentrated on the front half of the wing, so the A and B risers together carry roughly 80% of the total load. The C and D risers together see only the remaining 20%. This is why the C and D lines can be slack with moderate brake input — and also why aramid is preferred in lower, heavily-loaded line positions while Dyneema can be tolerated higher up where loads are small.
Loops and knots

Each line end has a loop that connects to either the riser or another line. Sewn loops are simple but introduce a stress concentration at the seam, reducing the line's effective strength. Spliced (looped) ends — where the line core is woven back through itself — keep nearly the full strength of the line. For unsheathed aramid lines, a special splicing technique is required to fit loops without significantly weakening them.
What happens when you change line lengths

Shortening or lengthening any line changes the wing's trim. Shortening the A-lines reduces the angle of attack — the wing flies faster but is more prone to collapse. Shortening the D-lines does the opposite: it increases angle of attack, slows the wing and brings it closer to stall. Shortening the B-lines on a school wing changes the brake-line geometry and the wing's behaviour in low-speed manoeuvres.

Trim-tabs allow you to vary line lengths in flight in a controlled way. Most trim-tab systems lengthen or shorten the C and D riser pair (sometimes also B), with the same overall effect as the speed bar but slower and finer.
Line ageing and check

Lines stretch over their lifetime; Dyneema also shrinks when not under load, especially when wet. UV exposure and repeated loading both degrade the core. With moderate brake input the D-lines may be loose (not under tension) — this is normal and means the wing is in a brake-induced angle-of-attack range where lift has shifted forward toward the leading edge. Sustained sagging beyond expectation, however, can indicate a stretch problem that warrants a workshop check. In a workshop check the lines are measured against the manufacturer specification and the wing is re-trimmed (typically with small tubes added to lengthen by 1-2 cm) so it flies like new again.

Routine line check before flight: scan each line from riser to wing for cuts, abrasions, missing sheath, or knots. A nick that exposes the core is enough to scrap the line. Lines that have been heavily exposed to sun and seawater age much faster than the wing itself; on coastal or tropical wings, lines are often the first part to need replacement.

sections.title: Line know-how
items: Sheath protects and colour-codes; core carries load.
Dyneema = low stretch, slight shrinkage when unloaded (especially wet); Aramid = dimensionally stable, kink-sensitive, UV-sensitive. Manufacturers mix them by position.
A and B risers carry ~80% of the load; C and D only ~20%.
Spliced loops preserve strength; sewn loops weaken the line.
Never modify line lengths yourself — workshop check measures and re-trims with tubes.

chapters.title: Risers, controls and accelerator
chapters.subtitle: Where the lines meet the harness — and where you steer
chapters.intro: Risers, brakes and speed bar are the pilot's interfaces to the wing. Knowing what each one really does is what makes a flight feel like flying — not surviving.

sections.text: Risers and quick links

Risers are polyamide or polyester webbing strips, lettered A (front) through C or D (rear), matching the lines they carry. They are connected to the harness karabiners and to the lines via screw-locked quick links. Tighten quick links by hand and then add a final quarter turn with a wrench: no more, no less. Karabiners themselves are aluminium or steel; aluminium dents more easily against rocks but in long-term opening/closing tests actually shows slightly better strength than steel. Either way, replace karabiners about every five years.

In stable straight-and-level flight roughly 80% of the load is on the A/B risers and only 20% on C/D, because lift is concentrated forward. The D risers are therefore sometimes used as emergency steering if brake lines fail, and with moderate brake input the D-lines may even be slack.
The speed system (accelerator)

The speed bar is a foot-operated pulley system that shortens the A risers the most, the B risers less, and sometimes the C risers slightly. The net effect is to reduce the wing's angle of attack without significantly changing its profile — the wing flies faster. Pushing the bar to the full deflection takes the wing to its maximum speed Vmax. The foot bar is fitted to solo wings only: on a tandem the front passenger blocks the legs, so a tandem uses trimmers instead.

sections.caption: Trim — the equivalent of speed bar on tandems and some school wings.

sections.text: Trim tabs perform the same job as the speed bar but with a slower, finer adjustment that you set with your hand. They are common on tandems where a foot bar is impractical. Tabs engaged (slow): the wing is less prone to frontal collapses. Tabs released (fast): more prone to collapses. The big practical difference vs the foot bar is that the bar springs back the instant you release it, while a trimmer has to be actively reached up to and let off — but you can see the trim setting clearly by hand.
Brakes (steering lines)

Brake lines run from the trailing edge of each half-wing through pulleys on the rear riser to handles you hold in each hand. Pulling a brake increases the angle of attack on that side, slowing it and turning the wing toward the brake. Brake line length is factory-set — never modify it. With your arms fully up, the brake lines should still form a slight rearward arc; straight lines mean the brakes are too short and the wing is permanently slightly braked, with a stall risk in turbulence.

How to hold the brake handle: pass the handle once around your wrist but never push your hand all the way through the loop. In an emergency (especially in winter with gloves) you must be able to drop the brake instantly and reach the reserve handle. A hand trapped through the loop fights you when you need it least.
Best glide and worst glide

In calm air, the glide ratio is best with arms fully up and no accelerator — the wing is at its trim setting. Pulling the brakes shifts the wing toward the slow side of the polar; pressing the speed bar shifts it toward the fast side. Both extremes reduce glide ratio. In a headwind, however, applying moderate accelerator improves glide ratio over the ground despite the higher sink rate.

sections.title: Riser and control facts
items: A+B risers carry ~80% of the load; C+D only ~20%.
Speed bar shortens A most, B less, sometimes C — reduces AoA. Solo wings only.
Trim tabs (used on tandems): engaged = slower, less collapse-prone; released = faster, more collapse-prone.
Tighten quick links hand-tight plus a 1/4 turn. Karabiners are good for ~5 years.
Brake handle: wrist through once, hand NEVER through the loop. Best glide in still air: arms up, no accelerator.

chapters.title: The harness
chapters.subtitle: Seat, back protection, attachment geometry
chapters.intro: Your harness keeps you comfortable for hours, protects you in a hard landing and decides how much information you get from the wing. Three big decisions define its character.

sections.text: Anatomy of a harness

Webbing from your shoulders and around your hips and legs converges on two karabiners, one on each side. These karabiners attach to the risers. The leg straps are critical — if they are not fastened, a sudden pitch-up in flight can let you slip out of the seat through the leg openings, with fatal consequences. Always check leg straps on every pre-flight.

sections.caption: Standard harness — the classic seated configuration.
Hike-and-fly harnesses — lighter, simpler designs.

sections.text: Back protection

There are several back-protection concepts and each has a trade-off. (1) Foam protectors give full protection from the very first second — you are protected during launch, in flight and on landing — but they are heavy and bulky to pack. (2) Airbag protectors are light and small to pack, but they only fill once the airflow blows into them in flight, so they offer essentially no protection on a botched take-off. (3) Convertible harnesses turn into a rucksack to save you carrying a separate backpack, but the same light fabric and zippers are stressed daily, and a packed-down foam protector inside a rucksack-mode harness can be compressed enough that it no longer unfolds properly. (4) One manufacturer has solved the airbag trade-off with an inflatable harness that you blow up by hand or with a pump BEFORE launch, so you have full air protection from take-off; you let the air out again to pack it down to a small volume. (5) Hike-and-fly minimal-strap harnesses dispense with protection altogether — you sit in essentially a band of webbing — and require a separately mounted reserve, because there is nothing to integrate it into.
Sitting vs prone

A prone (pod) harness lets the pilot lie almost horizontally, which gives lower drag and a measurably better glide; it also shelters you from the wind on long flights and leaves room for a cockpit. The trade-offs are that you cannot look directly upward and you do not sit fully upright, so climbing in feels foreign and twist risk is higher in collapses. For students, a sitting harness is the right choice — the upward view, the upright seat and the simpler launch behaviour matter more than the small glide gain.
Cross-bracing and attachment geometry

A heavily cross-braced (lattice) harness is stable in roll and feels reassuring, but it dampens feedback from the wing and reduces the effect of weight steering. A harness with little or no cross-brace gives a livelier feel and easier weight steering at the cost of stability. The choice depends on the pilot's experience and preferred style.

sections.caption: Reclined (pod) harnesses — low drag, more twist risk.

sections.text: Karabiner geometry matters. Karabiners high and close together damp the turbulence transmitted to the wing — useful for relaxed flying. Karabiners low and far apart give the pilot more feedback, less risk of twisting on launch, and a stronger lean forward into the wing.
Pre-flight: always check that both karabiners are correctly screwed shut and that the leg straps and chest strap are buckled. Always try a harness before buying — fit and feel are very personal.

sections.title: Harness choices
items: Foam: protects from second zero, heavy/bulky. Airbag: light/small, NO protection at launch.
Convertible (rucksack-mode): light to carry, but the fabric is stressed and the packed protector can lose loft.
Pre-inflated air harness: full protection from take-off, deflates to small volume to pack.
Hike-and-fly minimal strap: no protection, reserve must be mounted separately.
Prone (pod) harness: better glide, wind shelter, room for cockpit — NOT for school (no upward view, climb-in is awkward, higher twist risk).

chapters.title: The reserve parachute
chapters.subtitle: Last-line redundancy
chapters.intro: A reserve only saves you if it's correctly fitted, regularly repacked, and thrown without hesitation when you need it. Every pilot must know how their own reserve deploys.

sections.text: Reserve canopy types

Most modern reserves are cross-cell canopies (semi-square shapes built like a paraglider but with very loose weave). Their big advantage is that they are pendulum-stable in descent — you come down vertically without swinging. Older round (hemispherical) canopies still exist but are slowly being replaced because they tend to develop a pendulum motion that adds energy at the moment of touchdown. Steerable triangle or square reserves let an experienced pilot fly the reserve after deployment, but they require active piloting on top of an already stressful situation, so for a student a cross-cell reserve is the right answer.
Attachment to the harness

The reserve must attach high on the shoulder straps of the harness so that, when it deploys, it pulls the pilot upright into the suspended position under the open canopy. Attaching the reserve bridle to the riser karabiners instead leaves the pilot seated in the harness — a poor body position for the deceleration and landing roll. Reserve handle placement should always allow the pilot to find and grasp the handle with one hand in any flight orientation. Hike-and-fly minimal-strap harnesses cannot integrate a reserve and require a separate front-mount or chest-mount container.

sections.caption: Reserve parachute — bridle, container and connection.
Reserve parachute — geometry and packing.

sections.text: Reserve fabric and target sink

Reserve fabric is the same nylon as the wing but woven much finer and looser — it is deliberately ALLOWED to stretch, both fabric and Dyneema lines, because that stretch is what absorbs the opening shock. The target descent rate of a deployed reserve is about 5.5 m/s, which in pure energy terms is equivalent to jumping from a height of 1.5 m. But you are not just landing yourself: you also have the harness, the wing, helmet and clothes attached, and in practice the actual impact feels more like jumping from 2 to 3 m. Manufacturers know this — the certification drop test for a reserve is done from a bridge or a helicopter with a weight attached and WITHOUT the paraglider hanging below it, so the lab figure is a best case. In the real world the still-connected paraglider can interfere with the reserve canopy, swing the system and raise the sink rate further.
Landing technique

The single most important thing after a reserve toss is to land on your feet. Your legs are a better impact absorber than any harness protector — no foam or airbag is as good. Aim for feet first, knees and ankles flexed, and roll through your body to spread the impact (the classic parachute landing fall, PLF). Sitting down onto the harness protector at the moment of impact is a recipe for an injured spine; the protector is a last-resort backup, not a target.
Pull-down-apex and opening time

A pull-down apex design (a cord that pulls the centre of the parachute downward in flight) lowers the sink rate but makes the canopy slightly less stable. Opening time improves with low fabric porosity, short repack intervals, more relative airflow at deployment, and smaller canopy area. It worsens with high porosity, long intervals between repacks, slow airspeed at deployment, and large canopy area.
Repacking

Repack the reserve every 4 to 6 months, with 24 hours of airing before each repack. The whole system (container, bridle, canopy) must be checked at the same time. Skipping repack intervals is one of the most common causes of slow or failed deployments — the carefully folded fabric inside the container slowly compresses over time, making the eventual extraction harder.
Deployment sequence

The standard deployment sequence is: locate the handle, grasp it firmly, pull it fully out of the container, identify free air to the rear, and throw with full force. Once the reserve is open, neutralise the main wing by pulling it toward you on the B or C risers so that it does not interfere with the reserve canopy. Reserve deployment is required when the wing is uncontrollable or after a collision.

sections.title: Reserve discipline
items: Cross-cell = pendulum-stable, recommended for everyone. Round = older, pendulum-prone. Steerable = experts only.
Attach high on shoulder straps — pilot must hang upright.
5.5 m/s = ~1.5 m drop in theory; with paraglider+harness still attached, expect 2-3 m.
Land on your FEET. The protector is never as good as your legs.
Repack every 4-6 months; 24 h airing each time.
Sequence: locate -> grasp -> pull out -> look -> throw hard, then neutralise main wing on B/C.

chapters.title: Additional equipment and instruments
chapters.subtitle: Variometer, altimeter, GPS, anemometer
chapters.intro: A small set of instruments expands your decision-making in flight. Each one has a specific job — and a specific limitation worth knowing.

sections.text: Altimeter

An altimeter reads altitude by measuring static air pressure and converting it via the standard atmosphere model. Its setting matters: with QNH it shows altitude above mean sea level; with the standard 1013.25 hPa setting it shows Flight Levels. Because pressure varies day to day, a reading taken in the morning at a high-pressure site will drift as a depression approaches: if the high is weakening, the altitude reading at a fixed location will gradually increase over the day even though you have not moved.
The altimeter is the basic safety instrument for respecting airspace limits. Before any flight, set the QNH from a nearby reported airfield or from your own measurement at a known point of elevation.
Variometer

A variometer tells the pilot the vertical speed — climb or sink — by detecting changes in static pressure over a short time interval. It is the pilot's primary instrument for finding and centring thermals. Modern varios usually include altimeter and GPS functions, audible tones (rising pitch for lift, falling for sink) and configurable thresholds. Some advanced varios add total-energy compensation and wind calculation.
Anemometer and GPS

An anemometer measures wind speed — typically a small handheld unit used at take-off and landing fields. It does not measure direction directly; you read direction from windsocks, vegetation movement or simply feel. A GPS gives ground speed, ground track, and (combined with vario data) wind speed and direction in flight. None of these instruments measures airspeed directly on a paraglider — airspeed has to be inferred from the wing's behaviour or estimated from ground speed and known wind.

sections.title: Instrument basics
items: Altimeter: from static pressure; check the setting.
Variometer: vertical speed; thermals up, sink down.
Anemometer: handheld wind speed at take-off/landing.
GPS: ground speed and track; airspeed must be inferred.

chapters.title: Certification and care
chapters.subtitle: Categories, EN homologation and routine checks
chapters.intro: Certification labels are not marketing — they tell you what the wing has been tested for, and what experience it expects from you.

sections.text: Paraglider categories

Paragliders are grouped into categories that reflect the demands they make on the pilot. EN A wings are the most forgiving — designed for school use and beginners: if anything disturbs the wing and the pilot simply lets go, the wing returns to straight flight on its own. EN B wings are aimed at recreational pilots after their licence — typically after a few hundred flights on a school wing — and still have good passive safety with a step up in performance. EN C is for more experienced pilots — quicker, more demanding, more useful performance. EN D and CCC are competition-class wings: the pilot is expected to react instantly and intuitively to a disturbance without time to think.

sections.caption: Paraglider categories — from school wings to competition.
EN homologation — the European certification scheme.

sections.text: Performance vs safety in real numbers

What does the safety/performance trade-off look like in practice? From a launch at 1000 m above the valley in calm air, a modern school wing reaches a landing field about 8 km away. A competition wing — same launch, same conditions — reaches about 12 km. The school-wing number is also a generational improvement: just a few years ago even good school wings were down at 6 to 7.5 km. So wings today are noticeably better gliders AND noticeably safer than they used to be.
What EN testing actually does

EN homologation is a multi-stage test. First the wing is shock-loaded behind a moving vehicle (with a defined breaking point); then it is ramped up to at least 8G of static load (modern wings often reach 12G, acro wings around 16G); the structure and lines are inspected, the line strength is checked against the manufacturer's specification and a knot test is run. Then the test pilot flies 24 manoeuvres — collapses, stalls, spirals, asymmetric inputs — and the wing is scored on 90 individual points. Each manoeuvre may be flown up to five times, and a single occurrence in the target category counts toward the rating (a rule schools criticise, because the wing's worst behaviour over five attempts is not what shows up on the label). Brake-travel ranges are also measured: a school wing typically uses 0 to 50 cm of brake travel before stall, a competition wing only 0 to 30 cm.

sections.caption: The road to certification — what a wing goes through.

sections.text: Who decides what

EN is the standard worldwide. The only major exception is Germany, which still uses the older LTF / DHV system; otherwise EN A-B-C-D is what you will see on the certification label. The SHV/FSVL accepts both EN and LTF for Swiss flights. Wing inspection intervals are specified by the manufacturer, typically every 24 months or every 200 flight hours, whichever comes first. The owner is responsible for having inspections done on time — flying an overdue wing voids the certification and the insurance.
General care

Friction (dragging the wing along the ground, lines rubbing each other) is mechanical wear and should be minimised. UV is unavoidable in the air but easy to limit on the ground: pack the wing as soon as you land and keep it in the shade. Moisture on its own is not catastrophic if you dry the wing the same day — the disaster scenarios are a wet wing left in its bag for weeks (mould, coating damage) or a wet wing baking in a hot car (the oven effect is very destructive). Acid from a landing-field cow pat attacks the coating; rinse it off with lukewarm water. Cleaning agents and detergents are NEVER acceptable — they attack the coating directly. Always get advice from a flight school when choosing equipment: schools know the personality of each wing far better than the certification letter does.

sections.caption: Stress on the equipment — where wear concentrates.

sections.title: Certification reminders
items: EN A->D (LTF in Germany). EN A = school, EN B after a few hundred flights, EN C/D demand intuitive reactions.
Real numbers from 1000 m: school wing ~8 km, competition ~12 km. School wings used to be 6-7.5 km a few years ago.
8G minimum static load (acro ~16G). 24 manoeuvres, 90 points; 5 attempts per manoeuvre, one occurrence in category counts.
Brake travel: school wing 0-50 cm, competition 0-30 cm.
Owner is responsible for inspections at the manufacturer's interval; overdue = uncertified = uninsured.

books.title: Flight Practice
books.subtitle: Physiology, preparation, take-off, soaring, incidents and landing.

chapters.title: Physiology and first aid
chapters.subtitle: Hypoxia, wind chill and the ABC of rescue
chapters.intro: Free flight puts a small but unfamiliar set of demands on the human body. Knowing the warning signs of hypoxia, the cost of wind chill, and the standard accident response can save your life or a friend's.

sections.text: Hypoxia

Three forms of hypoxia matter for pilots. Instantaneous hypoxia begins from about 4000 m AMSL: the first symptom is often euphoria — a deceptive sense of well-being — followed by fatigue, slowed thinking, convulsions, coma and death by about 7000 m. Acute mountain sickness develops over 5 to 10 hours after climbing to 2800–3500 m: headache, nausea, occasional edema. Chronic hypoxia is the adaptation seen in long-term high-altitude residents — not the pilot's problem on a normal flight.

The treatment for hypoxia is one thing only: lose altitude rapidly. Sensitivity to hypoxia is increased by lack of fitness, medication, alcohol, tobacco, fatigue and lack of sleep. It is reduced by endurance training and by gradual acclimatisation over days to weeks. A healthy resting body can typically adapt to changes up to about 4000 m AMSL without issue.
Pressure equalisation

Sinus and ear pressure pain during altitude change occur when the Eustachian tubes cannot equalise pressure — typically because of a cold, sinusitis or ear infection. A pilot flying with a heavy cold and middle-ear infection risks barotrauma, severe pain and damage to the eardrum. The safe rule is to not fly with any condition affecting your sinuses or ears.
Wind chill and hydration

Long exposure to airflow accelerates the body's heat loss far beyond the air temperature alone. At 32 km/h flight speed in 2 °C ambient air, the wind chill on exposed skin is equivalent to a still-air temperature of about –11 °C. Heat loss raises oxygen consumption and lowers physical and mental performance. Airflow also accelerates fluid loss — hydrate properly during long flights.
Accident response: ABC

The standard sequence at an accident site is Airway, Breathing, Circulation — check whether the casualty is conscious, breathing and bleeding. Suspect a spinal injury whenever there is back pain or limb numbness: in those cases, do not move the casualty, since wrong handling can cause permanent paralysis. An unconscious casualty who is breathing should be placed in the recovery position to prevent inhalation of vomit.

Signs of severe shock include pale skin, bluish lips, cold sweat, weak rapid pulse and partial unresponsiveness. For helicopter rescue, all flying activity in the area must stop and any loose fabric — paragliders, clothing — must be cleared from the landing zone before the helicopter approaches.

Carrying a basic first-aid kit, a charged mobile phone with the European emergency number 112 and the Swiss alpine rescue number 1414, and warm clothing for an injured friend, are simple precautions every alpine pilot should take. After any non-trivial accident, document the conditions and circumstances while they are fresh — they help both the official investigation and your own learning.

sections.title: Physiology key points
items: Hypoxia from ~4000 m — first sign often euphoria; treat by descending.
Fly with a head cold = barotrauma risk.
32 km/h in 2 °C ≈ –11 °C wind chill.
ABC; immobilise suspected spine injuries; recovery position for unconscious breathing.

chapters.title: Flight preparation
chapters.subtitle: Weather, airspace, terrain and wildlife — at home and on site
chapters.intro: Pilots like to say a flight begins at the kitchen table. Four areas need to be checked before you leave home, and the inspection continues at the take-off and landing fields.

sections.text: Four pre-flight factors

Good preparation at home covers four areas. Weather: synoptic situation, local forecast, expected wind, instability and risk of thunderstorms. Legal and airspace situation: active LS-R, DABS, MIL hours and any temporary changes. Obstacles and terrain: cables, antennas, terrain steepness, possible alternate landings. Wildlife: nesting areas, seasonal restrictions for chamois and ibex. All four interact — a weather change can suddenly turn a previously safe landing into a hazardous one.

sections.caption: Flight preparation at home — weather, airspace, terrain, wildlife.

sections.text: QNH and pressure

QNH is the sea-level-corrected pressure reading reported by an airport. For example, Zurich-Kloten at about 432 m AMSL might read 950 hPa actual but 1015 hPa QNH. The pressure difference between stations indicates the slope of the isobars — a north-south difference between Zurich and Lugano of more than 4 hPa, with higher pressure in the north, is the classic threshold for Nordföhn risk in Ticino. Higher pressure in the south is the threshold for Südföhn risk on the north side of the Alps.
Documents and bulletins

The most important reference documents for flight planning are the Swiss Glider Chart (airspaces, hours, frequencies, cable obstacles), the AIP, the VFR-NOTAM (active CTRs/TMAs), the KOSIF (military shooting framework) and the DABS. None of them replaces the others — each carries information the others do not. Cross-check all of them before any flight in unfamiliar terrain.
On site at the flying area

On arrival, pre-inspect the take-off and landing fields, identify possible landing wind directions, and note all obstacles. No flight registration is required, but you should be familiar with cable and antenna positions, ridge profiles and obstacles that may channel wind. Pilots like to say the flight starts at the landing field, because that is where you assess wind, traffic and alternate options before going up to launch.

sections.caption: The flight starts at the landing field — assess wind and obstacles first.
The flight starts at the launch — final inspection and pre-flight check.

sections.text: Respecting wildlife

Golden eagles in the Alps nest between March and May. Avoid flying near nesting cliffs in spring. A territorial golden eagle marks its area with a characteristic undulating flight. Eagles mature at five years and remain monogamous for life, defending a territory of about 100 km². Chamois and ibex live on south slopes in winter and spring, and retreat to shaded cliffs in summer — keep distance especially in cold months when their energy reserves are critical.
The main danger

Across years of accident data, the strongest and most prevalent danger for paraglider pilots is strong wind combined with turbulence — especially in alpine terrain. Conservative wind limits, early decisions and the willingness to walk down are the single most important habits of long-flying pilots. No flight is worth a serious injury.

sections.title: Preparation crib
items: Check weather, airspace, obstacles, wildlife — all four.
QNH 1015 = pressure reported at sea-level reference.
Δp Zurich–Lugano > 4 hPa = Föhn risk.
Eagles nest Mar–May; chamois/ibex on south slopes Dec–Apr.

chapters.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch
chapters.subtitle: Five points, then inflate, check, accelerate
chapters.intro: More incidents happen on launch than in any other phase of flight. A disciplined five-point check and the three-phase launch sequence remove most of the guesswork.

sections.text: Five-point pre-flight check

The five-point check must be performed in order, every flight. First, equipment: harness leg straps closed, chest strap closed, helmet on, reserve handle in place and unobstructed. Second, risers and lines: lines untwisted and free, brake handles in hand, risers correctly attached. Third, wing: shape correct, no twists, leading edge open and facing the wind. Fourth, wind: direction, speed, gusts and consistency. Fifth, airspace: take-off and approach lanes clear of other traffic.

sections.caption: The five-point pre-flight check — in this order, every flight.

sections.text: Laying out the wing

Lay the wing in a slight arc, not a straight line, so that the centre lines tension first when you raise it. This gives a symmetric inflation. Sort the lines so that brakes are accessible and risers are not crossed. In strong wind, lay out smaller and more carefully — strong-wind launches succeed or fail in the first second.
Three-phase launch

Every paraglider launch has the same three phases. Phase 1: inflation and raising the wing — pull the A risers to inflate the cells and let the wing come up overhead. Phase 2: check and correct — verify the wing is symmetric, level, fully inflated, and that no lines are knotted. Phase 3: accelerate and lift off — commit forward, accelerate to take-off speed, and let the wing lift you off.

sections.caption: Three-phase launch — inflate, check, accelerate.

sections.text: Decision line and life line

Each launch slope has two virtual lines. The decision line is the last point at which you can comfortably abort: if the wing is not right by this point, stop. The point of no return — sometimes called the life line — is the last point before you must be airborne; beyond it, you cannot safely stop. The abort decision must be made before phase 3 begins.
Wind for take-off

Ideal headwind for a forward launch is around 15 km/h. A light tailwind of more than 5 km/h should normally cause an abort: ground speed is too high for safe ground handling and there is insufficient relative airflow over the wing. A sidewind requires inflating the wing into the wind and then pivoting toward the slope. Strong headwind of 20 to 25 km/h calls for a reverse (back-to-slope) launch; be aware of cliffs or steep drops behind you, as strong wind can pull you backward.

Just after launch, you may have to brake hard on one side to fly straight, which is a sign that the wing is asymmetric — either through a line problem or wing damage. Investigate before flying further. After a successful clean launch, wait until safely clear of the slope before relaxing into the harness or making large pilot inputs.

sections.title: Launch discipline
items: Five-point check in order: equipment, risers/lines, wing, wind, airspace.
Arc layout; centre lines tension first.
Phases 1→2→3: inflate → check → accelerate.
Abort decision before phase 3.

sections.text: After take-off, brake hard on ONE side to stay straight = wing is asymmetric (knot / damage). Stabilise and investigate — never relax.
Knot escalation: brake-impulse on knot side, then pull stabilo line, then induced 50% collapse — reserve over open meadow if still unsteerable.

chapters.title: Take-off — special conditions
chapters.subtitle: Wet wing, altitude, knots and after take-off
chapters.intro: A normal launch follows a predictable script. Wet wings, thin air or a hidden line knot rewrite that script — and the response has to be in your muscle memory.

sections.text: Wet wing

A wet wing weighs several kilograms more than a dry one. It is harder to inflate, needs a longer run and is more prone to stall during the take-off run. Its stability characteristics in stable flight, however, are not significantly changed once you are airborne. Avoid launching a wet wing in marginal conditions: the stall risk is concentrated in the launch itself and the first seconds of flight.
High-altitude take-off

Air is thinner at altitude. To generate the same lift the wing needs a higher airspeed — which means a faster run and a longer take-off distance. Plan for it: choose a steeper slope, accept a longer ground roll, and do not over-brake on lift-off. At very high altitudes, even moderate fitness can be enough to keep the airspeed needed for a clean launch.
Knots in the lines

A knot on the rear (D) lines raises the angle of attack on the affected side. The wing turns toward the knotted side and the affected side is close to stall: the consequence is a risk of full or parachutal stall on that side. To fly straight you must brake hard on the opposite side — and even then the wing may behave unpredictably.

A knot on the front (A) lines reduces the angle of attack on that side. The wing is prone to collapse on the affected side. The consequence is a sudden asymmetric collapse soon after launch, with a turn toward the knotted side. In both cases, if altitude allows, land as soon as possible to inspect and clear the knot.
After take-off

Once you are airborne, wait until you are safely clear of the slope before releasing the brakes to slide back into the seated position of the harness. Sliding into the harness near terrain leaves no time to recover from a turbulence event. Maintain straight flight away from the launch and look around for other traffic before you turn.

A launch in a forest clearing or gap deserves special care: the surrounding trees produce strong mechanical turbulence on the lee side, and a sudden lift just at the edge of the clearing can produce an over-pitched wing. Be ready with the brakes, accelerate firmly through the lift band and avoid hesitating once committed.

sections.caption: After take-off — clear the slope before relaxing into the harness.

sections.title: Special-case rules
items: Wet wing: heavier, longer run, more stall-prone on take-off.
High altitude: longer run, higher airspeed needed.
D-knot → turns toward knot, stall side.
A-knot → turns toward knot, collapse side.

chapters.title: Range, speed-to-fly and lateral drift
chapters.subtitle: Going far, staying up, holding a track
chapters.intro: Once airborne, every decision is a trade-off between airtime, distance and drift. Polar logic and the crab angle are the tools that make those decisions almost automatic.

sections.text: Far vs long

To fly the farthest in calm air, fly at maximum glide ratio — usually arms up, occasionally a very light brake. To stay airborne the longest, fly at minimum sink — typically 20 to 30 percent brake. Maximum distance equals glide ratio multiplied by altitude lost: a glide ratio of 6 from 1200 m gives 7.2 km.

sections.caption: Flight technique — speeds for each objective.

sections.text: Aerodynamic position

Pilot drag is a significant fraction of total system drag. The best aerodynamic position is reclined, with arms down behind the risers, elbows in close to the body, and legs symmetric and tucked. Crossed or extended legs add drag and reduce glide ratio. The position matters most at speed: when flying fast, the pilot's shape is a major contributor to total drag.
Speed-to-fly in wind and lift

In headwind or sinking air, fly faster — apply moderate accelerator — to retain a reasonable glide ratio over the ground. In tailwind, fly slower (light brake). In rising air, fly minimum sink to maximise the time spent in the lift. These four rules summarise everything in the speed-polar chapter applied to the real conditions you meet.
Crab angle

Crosswind pushes the wing sideways. To track a chosen line over the ground you must angle the wing's heading slightly into the wind — the crab angle. The stronger the crosswind, the larger the crab angle required. The higher your airspeed, the smaller the crab angle needed for a given wind. Always set the crab angle; do not chase the heading by repeatedly correcting it.

sections.caption: Dog leg path and the crab angle — flying with crosswind.

sections.text: Thermalling and wind

Thermal columns drift downwind. The best lift is found upwind of the visible cumulus marker, while the strongest sink is downwind of it. When circling in a thermal, the climb rate changes around the circle: if it is increasing, ease the turn; if it is decreasing, tighten it. If you lose the thermal entirely, a sharp 180° turn back to where it was usually re-finds it.

You can estimate the wind speed from your drift while circling: a drift of 3 km in 10 minutes corresponds to a wind of about 18 km/h. On cross-country flights, only leave a thermal once the next target is chosen and you have enough altitude to reach it. Wake turbulence (wingtip vortices) trails any glider — be careful when flying close behind another, especially near the ground.

On a long flight, a few habits add up. Keep a running mental list of alternate landings reachable from your current altitude. Track the wind direction from drift, vegetation and water surfaces, and update it every time you climb. Drink water regularly — dehydration makes you slow and irritable. And keep a margin: an attempted glide that just barely works in still air will fail with the slightest unexpected sink.

sections.title: Speed to fly and drift
items: Far → max glide; long → min sink.
Headwind/sink: faster. Tailwind/lift: slower.
Crab angle grows with crosswind, shrinks with airspeed.
Climb increasing → ease turn; decreasing → tighten.

chapters.title: Ridge soaring and thermalling
chapters.subtitle: The two main ways to stay up
chapters.intro: Soaring is what makes the sport. Two situations dominate: lift along a ridge, and rising bubbles of warm air. The geometry and the rules are different — and so are the dangers.

sections.text: Ridge soaring

Wind hitting a ridge is forced upward; if it is strong enough and the ridge is steep enough, the lift is great enough to sustain a glider. The pilot flies back and forth along the ridge in the lift band on the windward side. The key rule: always turn away from the ridge into the wind — never turn toward the ridge, where loss of altitude could put you into rotor or rock.

sections.caption: Ridge soaring — fly the windward face, turn into the wind.

sections.text: Right-of-way at the ridge is governed by a simple rule: the aircraft with the ridge on its right has priority. The other pilot must yield by turning away. This rule applies regardless of climb rates and avoids head-on conflicts when many pilots are working the same lift band.
Thermalling

A thermal is a column or bubble of warmer air rising from the ground. A pilot finds one, banks the wing and circles inside it, using the climb to gain altitude. Climb rate is rarely uniform around the circle — adjust by tightening or easing the turn as described in the previous chapter. Use a moderate bank angle: too flat and you lose contact with the core; too steep and the load factor climbs (with stall risk on the inside wingtip).

sections.caption: Thermalling — circle the column of rising air.

sections.text: In an occupied thermal, all gliders circle the same way — the direction chosen by the first pilot to enter. Joining in the opposite direction is forbidden and is the cause of most thermal collisions. Watch other pilots inside and outside the thermal at all times. Lost the thermal? A sharp 180° turn back to where it was is usually the quickest way to re-find it.

sections.title: Soaring discipline
items: Ridge: turn AWAY from the ridge, into the wind.
Ridge right-of-way: ridge on your right = priority.
Thermal: same direction as the first arrival.
Watch climb rate around the circle — ease or tighten.

sections.text: Soaring: terrain clearance, terrain clearance, terrain clearance — turbulence and other pilots are unpredictable when working a band.
Thermal centring uses lots of WEIGHT-SHIFT, not just brake; many pilots over-use brake and under-use weight.
Crisp alpine thermals from steep faces release fast — narrow, tight cores; flatland thermals are broader. Match bank angle to thermal width.
Only leave a thermal once you have chosen the next target AND have enough altitude to reach it.

chapters.title: Flight incidents and recovery
chapters.subtitle: Collapses, stalls, spirals and parachutal flight
chapters.intro: Every paraglider pilot meets turbulence-induced collapses sooner or later. Knowing what each event looks like and exactly how to respond is the difference between a corrected incident and an accident. Each manoeuvre below also has a 'how to come OUT of it' that is usually more dangerous than the entry — pay equal attention to the exit.

sections.text: Spiral dive

A spiral dive is a sequence of tight 360s that produces a very rapid loss of altitude without deforming the wing. It is a controlled descent — but at high G-forces, with a real risk of blackout (visual greyout from blood pooling). Exit by applying outside brake and shifting body weight toward the outside of the turn.

sections.caption: Manoeuvres and rapid-descent techniques.

sections.text: Big ears

Big ears are made by pulling the outer A lines on each side symmetrically. The wingtips collapse inward, reducing area and increasing sink to 3 to 4 m/s while keeping horizontal flight. There is no load-factor increase. On release, the wing returns to normal flight, with a slight parachutal stall risk during the transition. Combined with the speed bar, big ears give an even faster descent rate.
B-stall

Pulling the B risers symmetrically breaks the airflow on the upper surface; the wing stops flying horizontally and descends nearly vertically at over 5 m/s. There is no load-factor increase. The exit must be quick and decisive: release the B risers fully — slowly releasing them creates a parachutal-stall risk that can be hard to exit, especially close to the ground.
Full stall and parachutal stall

A full stall (brakes pulled too far) makes the wing lose its arch and deflate — extremely dangerous, only to be practised over water under SIV instruction. A parachutal stall is a state in which the wing keeps its shape but no air flows on the leading edge: brakes feel slack, lower surface goes slightly concave, vertical descent is high but airspeed is almost zero. Recover by pulling the A risers to surge the wing into normal flight; near the ground, prepare for a parachute landing.
Spin vs twist

A spin is a yaw rotation in which one half-wing goes backward — usually triggered by over-braking on the inside of a turn. Its signature is a sudden drop in brake tension on the inside. Recover by raising both arms fully. A twist is different: the pilot rotates relative to the wing — there is no flying problem with the wing itself, but you may be unable to steer until you untwist.
Collapses

An asymmetric (lateral) collapse: the wing turns toward the collapsed side. Compensate with brake on the open side and shift weight to the open side as well; too much brake on the open side can spin it. A frontal collapse: the leading edge is pushed in across most of the span. Pull and release both brakes briefly to re-inflate. If the pilot does nothing after a collapse of more than 50 percent of the span, the wing will typically turn into a spiral with continuing altitude loss.
Pendular dynamics and reserve

After a violent exit (extreme brake release after a stall, exit from a deep spiral) the wing first falls behind the pilot — at this moment release the brakes to avoid restalling — and then surges forward. The surge is checked with a brief but firm brake input to prevent a frontal collapse. The whole pendulum may take more than one swing to damp out.

Reserve deployment is indicated when the wing is uncontrollable or after a collision. Throw the reserve, then pull the B or C lines on the main wing to stop it from flying and tangling with the reserve. Sink rate under reserve is about 5 to 6 m/s — equivalent to a 1.5 m drop — and you should land with a parachute landing roll.

sections.caption: Reserve parachute — your second chance.

sections.title: Incident matrix
items: Spiral → outside brake + weight outside; blackout risk.
Big ears → outer A; sink 3–4 m/s, no G.
B-stall → both B risers; release quickly.
Asymmetric → brake & weight on open side; frontal → brake-release pulse.

sections.text: Brake grip variants

Every brake line has 10-15 cm of slack — needed because accelerated flight tilts the canopy forward and would otherwise pull the trailing edge taut. THREE grip variants exist. CLASSIC: brake handle held by the loop. Safe, used by all students, but the working range for thermal flying ends up uncomfortably low. FRENCH: hand passes through the loop, brake line held between thumb and index finger. Shortens brake travel and gives feel, but has been linked to fatal accidents — a gloved pilot may be unable to release the loop to reach the reserve. DO NOT use this grip with thick gloves. HUNGARIAN: hold the brake line between thumb and index finger without going through the loop. You can release at any time, but accidentally losing the line is easy. RECOMMENDED (Swiss school): take a SINGLE OUTWARD WRAP — turn the brake line outward over the heel of the hand, then hold it loosely between thumb and index finger. This eats the 10-15 cm slack, gives a short and sensitive working range, AND can be released in one motion to grab the reserve. For a full stall on an A-rated wing you may need two wraps.
Accelerated flight

Hands stay fully up in accelerated flight — never steer with the brakes. Pulling brake while the bar is in moves the trailing edge AND tilts the leading edge unfavourably, sharply raising collapse risk (frontal or asymmetric). Steer with WEIGHT SHIFT only; if that is not enough, add a very light pull (1-2 cm) on the rearmost riser. Set the accelerator by stepping with the heel into the first stirrup, pushing it forward, then bringing the second foot onto the next step. Push slowly and symmetrically until pulley reaches pulley on both sides (asymmetry will leave one side short). Come off slowly — the last few centimetres can snap out.
Pitching and rolling axes (training manoeuvres)

PITCHING (transverse axis, brake pulse): start slowly. Pull, pull, pull, pull — then BANG, hands up. When the wing surges forward, WAIT — let it pass through the dead point at the bottom AND start the climb on the next swing before braking again. Braking on the down-swing kills the energy you built. Each subsequent pulse can pull a bit deeper. To exit: catch the surge with a brief, firm braking that stops the wing about 45-60 degrees in front of you. Common error: pulling too fast and too deep too early — that stalls the wing. If you stall: hands UP and let the wing recover by itself; only respond to a collapse afterwards. ROLLING (longitudinal axis): same principle but the timing differs. Weight first, brake later. The body comes onto the new side BEFORE the brake — when the pilot reaches the highest point, weight shift is already on the new side; the new-side brake comes just after the lowest dead point (a much shorter wait than for pitching). Exit: skip one input; on the last swing, brake both sides as the wing comes back overhead.
Spiral with collapse held (advanced low-G descent)

An advanced descent technique combines an intentional asymmetric collapse on one side with a spiral turn on the open side. It produces a high sink rate with much LOWER G-forces than a normal spiral. Not part of the brevet exam, but worth knowing exists — those G-forces are what make a steep spiral dangerous (blackout, exit asymmetry). Train only under SIV instruction.

chapters.title: Landing
chapters.subtitle: Volte, final approach, height correction
chapters.intro: Every flight ends with a landing. The U-pattern approach is standard for a reason: it gives you a stable view of the wind, the field and your own height all the way down to the flare.

sections.text: The volte (U-approach)

The volte has four phases. Phase 1: descending 360° turns upwind or beside the landing field until you reach about 50–70 m. Phase 2: a downwind leg parallel to the field. Phase 3: a crosswind base leg. Phase 4: a final approach into the wind to the touchdown point. By convention the pattern is left-hand; the spirals in phase 1 also follow the volte direction.

sections.caption: Landing circuit — descending spirals into the volte.
Landing circuit — the four phases of the U pattern.

sections.text: Where to look

Throughout the approach the eyes are fixed on the landing target. Only brief glances are spent checking obstacles. Fixating on a single obstacle is a known cause of accidents — the wing tends to fly to where you are looking.
Adjusting height

If you are too high on the downwind leg, enlarge the crosswind base. If too low, shorten it. On final, too high: apply brakes and stand up to add drag — but watch out, over 80 percent brake risks a parachutal stall close to the ground. Too low on final: arms up for maximum glide, and brake only just before touchdown.
Strong wind, slope and difficult fields

Headwind makes the approach steeper but the ground speed lower — an easier landing. Tailwind has the opposite effect: flatter approach, higher ground speed — much harder, and to be avoided whenever possible. On a strong-wind landing (25 km/h or more) the priority after touchdown is to face the wing and collapse it: turn 180° to face the wing, run downwind toward it, and pull on the B or C risers to take its lift away. Never brake hard on landing in strong wind — the lift increase can drag you backward.

On sloping ground, land perpendicular to the slope contour or parallel to it. A landing facing straight up the slope is too brutal and risks injury. Avoid obstacles upwind of the landing field — mechanical turbulence from a building, line of trees or hangar extends many times the obstacle's height downwind.

sections.caption: Special landing situations — slope, water, trees.

sections.text: Multiple gliders and emergency landings

When several gliders approach the same landing field, the first (lowest) pilot picks the direction — by convention left. All following pilots must copy that direction, even if it feels 'wrong', to avoid collisions. After landing, leave the field immediately to make room for the next pilot.

Emergency landing priorities, best to worst: tree, sloped roof, water. Tree landings: stay roped or secured until rescue — falling from branches is the leading cause of injury after a tree landing. Water unavoidable: unclip the harness before touching the water. Power lines and crops are also better than open water.

sections.title: Landing rules
items: Eyes on the target; brief checks for obstacles.
Too high: enlarge base. Too low: shorten base.
Strong wind on touchdown: face the wing and collapse it.
Emergencies: tree > sloped roof > water; unclip before water contact.

sections.text: Position circle (phase 1) MUST turn in the SAME direction as the volte — right-hand pattern starts with right-hand spirals.
Too high on final = make yourself BIG (sit up out of harness, legs ready) for extra drag. Beware: >80% brake = parachutal stall close to the ground.
Slope landing: parallel to the contour or perpendicular, never straight up the slope.
Strong-wind landing: 180 degrees to face wing, run downwind toward it, pull B/C — never brake (lift increase will drag you).

chapters.intro: The Swiss brevet practical exam includes two compulsory flight figures, P1 and P2. Both are about orientation in space, controlling and exiting a bank angle, energy management — and about hitting the same axis you started on.

sections.text: P1 — Double circle

Two full 360 degree circles in the SAME direction, flown one straight after the other, entry and exit on the same axis (or parallel to it). Maximum 20 seconds. Start at MINIMUM SINK (both brakes pulled). Check airspace, then initiate the turn (the exam prescribes RIGHT). Set the bank angle with the INNER brake; if the bank is too steep, NEVER release the inner brake — pull the OUTER brake to reduce bank. Between the two circles the wing tends to pitch up and the bank slackens — pull a couple more centimetres of inner brake. Just before the second circle closes onto your heading, neutral body position, then pull the OUTER brake to brake both sides and stop on axis. Time stops only once the wing flies straight and stable.
P2 — Figure-eight along a guide line

Same principle as P1, but with a direction change: a full LEFT circle (always the first in the exam), then a full RIGHT circle, offset slightly along a guide line — looks like a shifted figure-eight. NOT a written '8' — both are CLOSED circles. Maximum 25 seconds. The most common error is initiating the first circle aggressively, doing an accidental wing-over on the change, then running out of energy. Cure: as you approach the axis at the end of the first circle, bring weight to neutral and pull the outer brake gently so you cross the axis BRAKED on both sides, then make the direct change into the second circle. Same exit as P1: outer-brake to neutral on axis, then release.
P1/P2 — common errors and the outer-brake rule

Three errors dominate both figures. (1) Releasing the inner brake to recover from too much bank — WRONG, this can spin the inner wing tip; keep inner brake, ADD outer brake instead. The outer brake is a fundamental tool in paragliding — also how you exit a spiral. (2) Not pulling enough inner brake to set the bank, then trying to fix it late in the circle. Train at minimum sink first, then progressively steeper. (3) Flying along the guide line between the two P2 circles. As soon as you have the axis, change direction — straight flight on the line wastes time.
